PLAINTIFF\'S INITIAL DISCLOSURE --- EXHIBIT 7

**EXCERPTS FROM**

**AMENDED AND RESTATED**

**LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY OPERATING AGREEMENT**

**OF**

**ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC**

**A Texas Limited Liability Company**

Effective as of September 1, 2016

As Amended Through March 1, 2021

The following excerpts from the Amended and Restated Limited Liability Company Operating Agreement of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (the \"Agreement\") are produced pursuant to the Court\'s Scheduling Order and the parties\' agreement regarding initial disclosures in *Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.*, Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex.). These excerpts include the Preamble, selected definitions from Article I, Article III (Members; Capital Contributions; Percentage Interests), Article IV (Management), selected provisions of Article IX (Transfer Restrictions), Article XII (Dissolution), and Exhibit A (Schedule of Members and Capital Contributions). Omitted sections are indicated by \"\[ \*\].\" Production Bates Nos. ARC-00004271 through ARC-00004312.

**AMENDED AND RESTATED** **LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY OPERATING AGREEMENT** **OF** **ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC**

**PREAMBLE**

THIS AMENDED AND RESTATED LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY OPERATING AGREEMENT (this \"Agreement\") of Arcadia Health Systems, LLC, a Texas limited liability company (the \"Company\"), is entered into and effective as of the 1st day of September, 2016, and amended and restated as of the 1st day of March, 2021, by and among the Members identified on **Exhibit A** attached hereto.

**RECITALS**

**WHEREAS**, the Company was organized as a Texas limited liability company by the filing of a Certificate of Formation with the Secretary of State of the State of Texas on August 15, 2016 (the \"Certificate of Formation\");

**WHEREAS**, the Members entered into the original Limited Liability Company Operating Agreement of the Company, dated September 1, 2016 (the \"Original Agreement\");

**WHEREAS**, the Members desire to amend and restate the Original Agreement in its entirety to reflect, among other things, the admission of Apex Medical Ventures, LP as a Member of the Company and certain modifications to the governance provisions hereof;

**NOW, THEREFORE**, in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and for other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Members hereby agree as follows:

**[ARTICLE I --- DEFINITIONS]{.underline}**

\[ \*\]

**\"Affiliate\"** means, with respect to any Person, any other Person that directly or indirectly Controls, is Controlled by, or is under common Control with, such Person.

\[ \*\]

**\"Capital Account\"** has the meaning set forth in Section 3.4.

**\"Capital Contribution\"** means the total amount of cash and the fair market value of any property contributed to the Company by a Member, as set forth on Exhibit A, as such Exhibit may be amended from time to time.

**\"Certificate of Formation\"** means the Certificate of Formation of the Company as filed with the Secretary of State of the State of Texas on August 15, 2016, as it may be amended or restated from time to time.

\[ \*\]

**\"Company\"** means Arcadia Health Systems, LLC, a Texas limited liability company.

\[ \*\]

**\"Control\"** (including the terms \"Controlling,\" \"Controlled by,\" and \"under common Control with\") means the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.

\[ \*\]

**\"Fiscal Year\"** means the calendar year or such other fiscal year as may be determined by the Managing Member.

\[ \*\]

**\"Majority-in-Interest\"** means Members holding, in the aggregate, more than fifty percent (50%) of the Percentage Interests then held by all Members.

**\"Managing Member\"** means Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, or such other Person as may be designated as Managing Member in accordance with Section 4.1.

**\"Member\"** means each Person identified as a member on Exhibit A hereto, as such Exhibit may be amended from time to time, and any Person admitted as an additional or substitute Member in accordance with this Agreement, in each case for so long as such Person holds a Percentage Interest in the Company. \"Members\" means all such Persons collectively.

\[ \*\]

**\"Percentage Interest\"** means, with respect to each Member, the percentage set forth opposite such Member\'s name on **Exhibit A** hereto, as such Exhibit may be amended from time to time, representing such Member\'s proportionate interest in the profits, losses, distributions, and voting rights of the Company.

**\"Person\"** means any individual, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, trust, estate, association, governmental authority, or other entity.

\[ \*\]

**\"Super-Majority Interest\"** means Members holding, in the aggregate, at least seventy-five percent (75%) of the Percentage Interests then held by all Members.

**\"Transfer\"** has the meaning set forth in Section 9.1.

\[ \*\]

**[ARTICLE III --- MEMBERS; CAPITAL CONTRIBUTIONS; PERCENTAGE INTERESTS]{.underline}**

**Section 3.1 --- Members.**

The Members of the Company as of the date of this Agreement are set forth on **Exhibit A** attached hereto and incorporated herein by reference. Each Member\'s name, address, Percentage Interest, and Capital Contribution are as set forth on Exhibit A.

**Section 3.2 --- Percentage Interests.**

\(a\) The Percentage Interests of the Members as of the date of this Agreement are as set forth on **Exhibit A**. The aggregate of all Percentage Interests shall at all times equal one hundred percent (100%).

\(b\) Percentage Interests shall be adjusted from time to time to reflect the admission of additional Members, the withdrawal or removal of Members, additional Capital Contributions, the Transfer of Membership Interests, or other events as provided in this Agreement.

\(c\) No Member shall be required to make any Capital Contribution to the Company other than the initial Capital Contribution set forth on Exhibit A, except as may be unanimously approved by all Members in writing.

**Section 3.3 --- Additional Capital Contributions.**

\(a\) The Managing Member may from time to time determine that additional Capital Contributions are necessary for the conduct of the Company\'s business. In such event, the Managing Member shall deliver written notice (a \"Capital Call Notice\") to each Member specifying (i) the aggregate amount of additional Capital Contributions required, (ii) each Member\'s pro rata share thereof based on such Member\'s Percentage Interest, and (iii) the date by which such Capital Contribution must be made, which date shall not be less than thirty (30) days after delivery of the Capital Call Notice.

\(b\) No Member shall be obligated to fund any additional Capital Contribution unless all Members unanimously consent in writing.

\[ \*\]

**Section 3.4 --- Capital Accounts.**

A separate Capital Account shall be established and maintained for each Member in accordance with Treasury Regulation Section 1.704-1(b)(2)(iv). Each Member\'s Capital Account shall be (a) credited with such Member\'s Capital Contributions, allocations of Net Profits, and any items of income or gain specially allocated to such Member, and (b) debited with distributions to such Member, allocations of Net Losses, and any items of loss or deduction specially allocated to such Member.

**Section 3.5 --- No Interest on Capital; No Return of Capital.**

No Member shall be entitled to receive interest on such Member\'s Capital Contributions or Capital Account balance. Except as expressly provided herein, no Member shall have the right to demand or receive a return of such Member\'s Capital Contributions.

**Section 3.6 --- Distributions.**

\(a\) The Managing Member shall determine the timing and amount of distributions to the Members, in her sole discretion, taking into account the Company\'s cash flow, working capital requirements, reserves for anticipated expenses and liabilities, and such other factors as the Managing Member deems relevant.

\(b\) All distributions shall be made to the Members in proportion to their respective Percentage Interests, unless otherwise unanimously agreed by the Members in writing.

\[ \*\]

**[ARTICLE IV --- MANAGEMENT]{.underline}**

**Section 4.1 --- Managing Member.**

\(a\) The Company shall be managed by a Managing Member. The initial Managing Member shall be Dr. Rachel Okonkwo. The Managing Member shall serve in such capacity until her resignation, removal, death, disability, or incapacity, as provided herein.

\(b\) The Managing Member shall have full, exclusive, and complete authority, power, and discretion to manage and control the business, affairs, and properties of the Company, to make all decisions regarding the same, and to perform any and all other acts or activities customary or incident to the management of the Company\'s business, except as otherwise expressly limited by this Agreement.

**Section 4.2 --- Specific Powers of Managing Member.**

Without limiting the generality of Section 4.1, the Managing Member shall have the power and authority, on behalf of the Company, to:

\(a\) acquire, hold, manage, operate, lease, and dispose of any real, personal, or mixed property;

\(b\) enter into, execute, amend, supplement, acknowledge, and deliver any contracts, agreements, or instruments necessary or appropriate to the conduct of the Company\'s business;

\(c\) borrow money and issue evidences of indebtedness, and secure any such indebtedness by mortgage, pledge, security interest, or other encumbrance on any assets of the Company;

\(d\) employ, engage, or contract with such Persons as the Managing Member deems necessary or appropriate for the conduct of the Company\'s business, including officers, employees, agents, attorneys, accountants, consultants, and other professionals;

\(e\) open and maintain bank accounts, investment accounts, and brokerage accounts in the name of the Company;

\(f\) make tax elections on behalf of the Company;

\(g\) institute, prosecute, defend, settle, compromise, and dismiss lawsuits, claims, and other proceedings in the name of and on behalf of the Company; and

\(h\) take all such other actions as are necessary, desirable, or convenient to carry out the business and purposes of the Company.

**Section 4.3 --- Actions Requiring Super-Majority Approval.**

Notwithstanding Section 4.2, the following actions shall require the prior written consent of a Super-Majority Interest:

\(a\) any merger, consolidation, conversion, or similar transaction involving the Company;

\(b\) the sale, lease, exchange, or other disposition of all or substantially all of the Company\'s assets;

\(c\) the voluntary dissolution and winding up of the Company;

\(d\) the admission of any new Member;

\(e\) any transaction between the Company and the Managing Member or any Affiliate of the Managing Member, other than the payment of compensation to the Managing Member as contemplated by Section 4.5;

\(f\) the incurrence of indebtedness by the Company in excess of Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$500,000) in the aggregate;

\(g\) any amendment to this Agreement; and

\(h\) any single capital expenditure or commitment in excess of One Million Dollars (\$1,000,000), or any series of related capital expenditures or commitments in excess of Two Million Dollars (\$2,000,000) in any Fiscal Year.

**Section 4.4 --- Officers.**

The Managing Member may appoint one or more officers of the Company (each, an \"Officer\"), including a President, Chief Executive Officer, Chief Technology Officer, Chief Financial Officer, Vice President, Secretary, and Treasurer, and may delegate to such Officers such authority and duties as the Managing Member deems appropriate. Officers shall serve at the pleasure of the Managing Member.

**Section 4.5 --- Compensation of Managing Member.**

The Managing Member shall be entitled to receive reasonable compensation for services rendered to the Company, as determined by the Managing Member; *provided, however*, that any compensation in excess of Three Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$300,000) per annum shall require the written consent of a Majority-in-Interest (excluding the Managing Member\'s vote for purposes of this Section 4.5).

\[ \*\]

**[ARTICLE IX --- TRANSFER RESTRICTIONS]{.underline}**

**Section 9.1 --- Restrictions on Transfer.**

\(a\) No Member shall sell, assign, pledge, hypothecate, encumber, or otherwise transfer or dispose of (each, a \"Transfer\"), directly or indirectly, all or any portion of such Member\'s Membership Interest, except in compliance with this Article IX. Any purported Transfer in violation of this Article IX shall be null and void and of no effect.

\(b\) No Transfer of a Membership Interest shall be effective unless and until (i) the transferee has been approved by a Majority-in-Interest (excluding the Transferring Member\'s vote), (ii) the transferee has executed a counterpart of this Agreement or a joinder agreement in form reasonably satisfactory to the Managing Member, and (iii) the Transferring Member and/or the transferee has paid all reasonable expenses incurred by the Company in connection with such Transfer.

**Section 9.2 --- Right of First Refusal.**

\[ \*\]

**Section 9.3 --- Permitted Transfers.**

Notwithstanding Section 9.1, a Member may Transfer all or any portion of such Member\'s Membership Interest without the consent of the other Members to:

\(a\) in the case of a Member that is an individual, (i) a trust for the benefit of such Member or such Member\'s spouse, children, or other descendants, (ii) such Member\'s spouse, children, or other descendants, or (iii) an entity wholly owned by such Member or such Member\'s spouse, children, or other descendants;

\(b\) in the case of a Member that is a partnership, limited liability company, corporation, or other entity, (i) any Affiliate of such Member, (ii) any partner, member, or shareholder of such Member, or (iii) any successor entity resulting from a merger, consolidation, or reorganization of such Member;

*provided* that in each case (x) the transferee executes a counterpart of this Agreement or a joinder agreement and (y) such Transfer would not cause the Company to be treated as a publicly traded partnership taxable as a corporation under Section 7704 of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, as amended.

\[ \*\]

**[ARTICLE XII --- DISSOLUTION]{.underline}**

**Section 12.1 --- Events of Dissolution.**

The Company shall be dissolved upon the earliest to occur of the following:

\(a\) the written consent of all Members to dissolve the Company;

\(b\) the entry of a decree of judicial dissolution under the Texas Business Organizations Code;

\(c\) the occurrence of any event that makes it unlawful or impossible for the business of the Company to be carried on; or

\(d\) the sale, lease, exchange, or other disposition of all or substantially all of the Company\'s assets, unless the Members elect to continue the Company by a Super-Majority Interest vote within ninety (90) days following such disposition.

**Section 12.2 --- Winding Up.**

Upon dissolution, the Managing Member (or, if there is no Managing Member, a Person appointed by a Majority-in-Interest) shall wind up the Company\'s affairs. The assets of the Company shall be applied and distributed in the following order of priority:

\(a\) first, to the payment of debts and liabilities of the Company (including any debts owed to Members in their capacity as creditors) and the expenses of liquidation;

\(b\) second, to the establishment of such reserves as the Person conducting the winding up deems reasonably necessary for any contingent or unforeseen liabilities or obligations of the Company;

\(c\) third, to the Members in proportion to their respective positive Capital Account balances, after giving effect to all allocations of Net Profits and Net Losses for the Fiscal Year in which the dissolution occurs; and

\(d\) fourth, to the Members in proportion to their respective Percentage Interests.

\[ \*\]

**[ARTICLE XIV --- MISCELLANEOUS]{.underline}**

\[ \*\]

**Section 14.3 --- Governing Law.**

This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Texas, without giving effect to any choice of law or conflict of law rules or provisions that would cause the application of the laws of any jurisdiction other than the State of Texas.

**Section 14.4 --- Notices.**

All notices, requests, demands, and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given if delivered personally, sent by overnight courier, or mailed by certified or registered mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested, addressed as follows:

> If to the Company:
>
> Arcadia Health Systems, LLC 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210 San Antonio, TX 78229
>
> Attn: Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, Managing Member
>
> If to a Member, to the address set forth on **Exhibit A** hereto.

**Section 14.5 --- Entire Agreement.**

UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS SAN ANTONIO DIVISION

**ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,** Plaintiff,

v\.

**MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,** Defendant.

**Civil Action No. \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

**(Removed from the 73rd District Court, Bexar County, Texas; Cause No. 2023-CI-14672)**

**DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.\'S NOTICE OF REMOVAL OF CIVIL ACTION PURSUANT TO 28 U.S.C. §§ 1332, 1441, AND 1446**

Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\"Meridian\" or \"Defendant\"), by and through its undersigned counsel, Stonebridge & Calloway LLP, hereby files this Notice of Removal of Civil Action pursuant to 28 U.S.C. §§ 1332, 1441, and 1446, and respectfully shows the Court as follows:

**[I. INTRODUCTION]{.underline}**

1\. On August 7, 2023, Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\"Arcadia\" or \"Plaintiff\") filed the above-captioned action in the 73rd District Court of Bexar County, Texas, Cause No. 2023-CI-14672 (the \"State Court Action\"), asserting claims for breach of contract, fraud, negligent misrepresentation, unjust enrichment, and violations of the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act (\"DTPA\"), Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §§ 17.41--17.63, arising from a Master Software License and Services Agreement (\"MSLSA\") entered between the parties on March 15, 2022.

2\. This Court has original jurisdiction over this action under 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a) because complete diversity of citizenship exists between Plaintiff and Defendant and the amount in controversy exceeds \$75,000, exclusive of interest and costs. Removal is therefore proper under 28 U.S.C. § 1441(a).

3\. Defendant was served with the Summons and Original Petition on August 10, 2023. This Notice of Removal is timely filed within thirty (30) days of service, as required by 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b)(1).

**[II. TIMELINESS OF REMOVAL]{.underline}**

4\. Pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b)(1), a notice of removal must be filed within thirty days after the defendant receives, through service or otherwise, a copy of the initial pleading. Meridian was served with the Summons and Original Petition on August 10, 2023. This Notice of Removal is filed on August 29, 2023, which is within the statutory thirty-day removal period.

5\. All defendants that have been properly joined and served have joined in or consented to removal. 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b)(2)(A). Meridian is the sole defendant in this action.

**[III. VENUE]{.underline}**

6\. Pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1441(a), this action is removed to the United States District Court for the Western District of Texas because the State Court Action was pending in Bexar County, Texas, which lies within the San Antonio Division of the Western District of Texas. *See* 28 U.S.C. § 124(d)(3).

7\. Defendant notes that the MSLSA contains a forum selection clause at Section 12.8 providing for exclusive jurisdiction in the federal or state courts located in Austin, Travis County, Texas. Defendant reserves the right to seek transfer to the Austin Division of this Court pursuant to the forum selection clause and/or 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a), but such transfer does not affect the propriety of removal to this district.

**[IV. BASIS FOR FEDERAL JURISDICTION]{.underline}**

**A. Diversity of Citizenship --- 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a)**

8\. This Court has original jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a) because there is complete diversity of citizenship between Plaintiff and Defendant, and the amount in controversy exceeds \$75,000, exclusive of interest and costs.

**B. Citizenship of Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.**

9\. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is a corporation organized and existing under the laws of the State of Delaware. For purposes of 28 U.S.C. § 1332(c)(1), a corporation is deemed a citizen of the state in which it is incorporated and the state where it has its principal place of business.

10\. Meridian is incorporated in the State of Delaware. Meridian\'s principal place of business is located at 9200 Research Boulevard, Suite 400, Austin, Texas 78759, where its corporate headquarters are situated and from which its executive officers, including Chief Executive Officer Thomas R. Whitfield and General Counsel Patricia M. Espinoza, direct, control, and coordinate the corporation\'s activities. Accordingly, under the \"nerve center\" test articulated by the Supreme Court in *Hertz Corp. v. Friend*, 559 U.S. 77, 92--93 (2010), Meridian\'s principal place of business is in Austin, Texas.

11\. Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is therefore a citizen of both Delaware and Texas for diversity jurisdiction purposes.

**C. Citizenship of Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC**

12\. Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC is a Texas limited liability company with its principal office located at 4500 Medical Parkway, Suite 210, San Antonio, Texas 78229. Arcadia was formed in 2016 under the laws of the State of Texas and conducts business as a regional healthcare IT services provider operating electronic health record integration and analytics platforms for hospitals and clinics across Texas and Louisiana.

13\. The managing member and chief executive of Arcadia is Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, who is domiciled in the State of Texas.

14\. Accordingly, Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC is a citizen of the State of Texas for purposes of diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332.

**D. Complete Diversity Exists Between the Parties**

15\. Defendant Meridian is a citizen of Delaware and Texas. Plaintiff Arcadia is a citizen of Texas. Because Defendant\'s Delaware citizenship is not shared by Plaintiff, complete diversity of citizenship exists between the parties as required by 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a) and *Strawbridge v. Curtiss*, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806).

**E. Amount in Controversy**

16\. The amount in controversy in this action exceeds the jurisdictional threshold of \$75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, as required by 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a).

17\. In the Original Petition, Plaintiff seeks total damages in the amount of \$47,300,000, comprised of the following categories:

> \(a\) Direct damages and restitution in the amount of \$14,700,000, representing the full contract value of the MSLSA;
>
> \(b\) Lost profits in the amount of \$18,400,000, projected over a five-year period based upon a damages analysis prepared by Plaintiff\'s financial advisor, Grayson Whitmore & Co.;
>
> \(c\) Increased operating costs in the amount of \$6,700,000, including alleged costs of maintaining Plaintiff\'s legacy system, additional IT staffing, and fees paid to Plaintiff\'s third-party systems integrator;
>
> \(d\) Reputational harm in the amount of \$5,000,000, based upon the alleged loss of two prospective hospital clients; and
>
> \(e\) Punitive damages under the DTPA treble damages provision in the amount of \$2,500,000.

18\. The sum of \$47,300,000 substantially exceeds the \$75,000 statutory threshold. The amount in controversy is determined from the face of the plaintiff\'s complaint. *See St. Paul Mercury Indem. Co. v. Red Cab Co.*, 303 U.S. 283, 288--89 (1938); *De Aguilar v. Boeing Co.*, 47 F.3d 1404, 1408 (5th Cir. 1995). Because Plaintiff\'s own pleading demands \$47,300,000 in total damages, the amount-in-controversy requirement is plainly satisfied.

**[V. COMPLIANCE WITH REMOVAL PROCEDURE]{.underline}**

**A. Filing with the Federal Court --- 28 U.S.C. § 1446(a)**

19\. This Notice of Removal is filed in the United States District Court for the Western District of Texas, the district and division embracing the place where the State Court Action is pending, as required by 28 U.S.C. § 1446(a).

20\. Pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1446(a), this Notice of Removal is accompanied by the following documents:

> \(a\) A true and correct copy of all process, pleadings, and orders served upon Defendant in the State Court Action, including the Original Petition, the Summons, and the Citation, attached hereto as **Exhibit A**;
>
> \(b\) A true and correct copy of the MSLSA and SOW-1 between the parties, attached as exhibits to the Original Petition, attached hereto as **Exhibit B**; and
>
> \(c\) A true and correct copy of Meridian\'s Certificate of Incorporation from the Delaware Secretary of State, attached hereto as **Exhibit C**.

**B. Notice to Adverse Parties and the State Court --- 28 U.S.C. § 1446(d)**

21\. Promptly after filing this Notice of Removal, Defendant will file a copy of this Notice with the Clerk of the 73rd District Court of Bexar County, Texas, and will serve written notice of the filing of this Notice upon Plaintiff and its counsel, as required by 28 U.S.C. § 1446(d).

22\. Upon filing of this Notice of Removal, the State Court Action shall be removed to this Court, and the state court shall proceed no further unless and until the case is remanded. 28 U.S.C. § 1446(d).

**C. Consent of All Defendants --- 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b)(2)(A)**

23\. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. is the sole defendant in this action. No other defendant has been named, served, or joined. Accordingly, the unanimity requirement of 28 U.S.C. § 1446(b)(2)(A) is satisfied.

**[VI. NO WAIVER OF DEFENSES]{.underline}**

24\. By filing this Notice of Removal, Defendant does not waive, and expressly preserves, any and all defenses, objections, affirmative defenses, rights, and privileges available to it under applicable law, including but not limited to:

> \(a\) All defenses available under Rule 12 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, including but not limited to failure to state a claim upon which relief can be granted under Rule 12(b)(6);
>
> \(b\) All contractual defenses, including enforcement of the limitation of liability provisions (Section 8.1), the consequential damages waiver (Section 8.2), the exclusive remedies provision (Section 8.3), the warranty disclaimer (Section 9.4), the integration clause (Section 12.1), and the forum selection clause (Section 12.8) of the MSLSA;
>
> \(c\) All affirmative defenses, including but not limited to the economic loss doctrine, contractual waiver, estoppel, failure to mitigate, comparative fault, and the statute of limitations;
>
> \(d\) The right to seek transfer to the Austin Division of this Court pursuant to the forum selection clause in Section 12.8 of the MSLSA and/or 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a); and
>
> \(e\) The right to seek enforcement of the jury trial waiver set forth in Section 12.9 of the MSLSA.

**[VII. CONCLUSION AND PRAYER]{.underline}**

25\. For the foregoing reasons, Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. respectfully submits that removal of this action from the 73rd District Court of Bexar County, Texas, to the United States District Court for the Western District of Texas is proper pursuant to 28 U.S.C. §§ 1332, 1441, and 1446. Complete diversity of citizenship exists between the parties, the amount in controversy exceeds \$75,000, and this Notice has been timely filed within the statutory period.

**WHEREFORE**, Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. respectfully requests that this Court:

> \(a\) Accept this Notice of Removal and assume jurisdiction over this action;
>
> \(b\) Set the matter for an initial scheduling conference at the Court\'s earliest convenience; and
>
> \(c\) Grant such other and further relief as the Court deems just and proper.

Respectfully submitted,

STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD

Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)

    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
"""Build the three deliverables for the Rule 12(b)(6) motion package."""
import re
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
from docx.enum.text import WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH, WD_LINE_SPACING
from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

FONT = 'Times New Roman'

def _set_run_font(run, size=12):
    run.font.name = FONT
    run.font.size = Pt(size)
    rpr = run._element.get_or_add_rPr()
    rfonts = rpr.find(qn('w:rFonts'))
    if rfonts is None:
        rfonts = OxmlElement('w:rFonts')
        rpr.insert(0, rfonts)
    for a in ('w:ascii', 'w:hAnsi', 'w:cs', 'w:eastAsia'):
        rfonts.set(qn(a), FONT)

def rich(p, text, bold=False, italic_default=False, size=12):
    """Add text to paragraph p, parsing *...* as italics."""
    parts = re.split(r'(\*[^*]+\*)', text)
    for part in parts:
        if not part:
            continue
        if len(part) > 2 and part.startswith('*') and part.endswith('*'):
            run = p.add_run(part[1:-1])
            run.italic = True
            run.bold = bold
        else:
            run = p.add_run(part)
            run.italic = italic_default
            run.bold = bold
        _set_run_font(run, size)

def base_style(doc):
    st = doc.styles['Normal']
    st.font.name = FONT
    st.font.size = Pt(12)

from docx.oxml.ns import qn
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement

FONT = 'Times New Roman'

def _set_run_font(run, size=12):
    run.font.name = FONT
    run.font.size = Pt(size)
    rpr = run._element.get_or_add_rPr()
    rfonts = rpr.find(qn('w:rFonts'))
    if rfonts is None:
        rfonts = OxmlElement('w:rFonts')
        rpr.insert(0, rfonts)
    for a in ('w:ascii', 'w:hAnsi', 'w:cs', 'w:eastAsia'):
        rfonts.set(qn(a), FONT)

def rich(p, text, bold=False, italic_default=False, size=12):
    """Add text to paragraph p, parsing *...* as italics."""
    parts = re.split(r'(\*[^*]+\*)', text)
    for part in parts:
        if not part:
            continue
        if len(part) > 2 and part.startswith('*') and part.endswith('*'):
            run = p.add_run(part[1:-1])
            run.italic = True
            run.bold = bold
        else:
            run = p.add_run(part)
            run.italic = italic_default
            run.bold = bold
        _set_run_font(run, size)

    st = doc.styles['Normal']
    st.font.name = FONT

# ============================ MOTION TO DISMISS ============================
def build_motion():
    doc = Document()
    base_style(doc); set_margins(doc); add_page_numbers(doc)
    case_no = 'Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA'
    title = "DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.'S MOTION TO DISMISS\nPLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO FEDERAL RULE OF\nCIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6)"
    # caption title is multi-line; render manually
    center(doc, 'UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT', bold=True)
    center(doc, 'WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS', bold=True)
    center(doc, 'AUSTIN DIVISION', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tPlaintiff,')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'v.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tDefendant.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, case_no)
    blank(doc)
    for ln in ["DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.'S MOTION TO DISMISS",
               "PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO FEDERAL RULE OF",
               "CIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6)"]:
        center(doc, ln, bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'Response Due: Twenty-one (21) days after the date of filing')
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr(); pbdr = OxmlElement('w:pBdr'); bottom = OxmlElement('w:bottom')
    bottom.set(qn('w:val'),'single'); bottom.set(qn('w:sz'),'6'); bottom.set(qn('w:space'),'1'); bottom.set(qn('w:color'),'auto')
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)

    # ---- Table of Contents ----
    heading(doc, 'TABLE OF CONTENTS', space_before=6, space_after=6)
    toc = [
        'I.    Introduction',
        'II.   Statement of Facts',
        'III.  Legal Standard',
        'IV.   Argument',
        '         A. Count V (DTPA) Is Barred by the Section 17.49(f) Transaction Exemption',
        '         B. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) Is Barred by the Express Contract',
        '         C. Count II (Fraud) Fails Under Rule 9(b), the Economic Loss Doctrine,',
        '            the Puffery Doctrine, and the Integration Clause',
        '         D. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) Fails for Lack of an Independent',
        '            Duty and Under the Economic Loss Doctrine',
        '         E. Count I (Breach of Contract) Is Barred by Deemed Acceptance, the Expired',
        '            Limited Warranty, and the Contractual Damages Limitations',
        'V.    Conclusion',
    ]
    for t in toc:
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
        rich(p, t)
    blank(doc)

    # ---- Table of Authorities ----
    heading(doc, 'TABLE OF AUTHORITIES', space_before=6, space_after=6)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)

    center(doc, 'AUSTIN DIVISION', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tPlaintiff,')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'v.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tDefendant.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, case_no)
    blank(doc)
    for ln in ["DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.'S MOTION TO DISMISS",
               "PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO FEDERAL RULE OF",
               "CIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6)"]:
        center(doc, ln, bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'Response Due: Twenty-one (21) days after the date of filing')
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr(); pbdr = OxmlElement('w:pBdr'); bottom = OxmlElement('w:bottom')
    bottom.set(qn('w:val'),'single'); bottom.set(qn('w:sz'),'6'); bottom.set(qn('w:space'),'1'); bottom.set(qn('w:color'),'auto')
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)

    # ---- Table of Contents ----
    heading(doc, 'TABLE OF CONTENTS', space_before=6, space_after=6)
    toc = [
        'I.    Introduction',
        'II.   Statement of Facts',
        'III.  Legal Standard',
        'IV.   Argument',
        '         A. Count V (DTPA) Is Barred by the Section 17.49(f) Transaction Exemption',
        '         B. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) Is Barred by the Express Contract',
        '         C. Count II (Fraud) Fails Under Rule 9(b), the Economic Loss Doctrine,',
        '            the Puffery Doctrine, and the Integration Clause',
        '         D. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) Fails for Lack of an Independent',
        '            Duty and Under the Economic Loss Doctrine',
        '         E. Count I (Breach of Contract) Is Barred by Deemed Acceptance, the Expired',
        '            Limited Warranty, and the Contractual Damages Limitations',
        'V.    Conclusion',
    ]
    for t in toc:
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
        rich(p, t)
    blank(doc)

    # ---- Table of Authorities ----
    heading(doc, 'TABLE OF AUTHORITIES', space_before=6, space_after=6)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)

        '*Presidio Enters., Inc. v. Warner Bros. Distrib. Corp.*, 784 F.2d 674 (5th Cir. 1986) .. 12',
        '*Benchmark Elecs., Inc. v. J.M. Huber Corp.*, 343 F.3d 719 (5th Cir. 2003) ...... 7, 10, 15',

        '    246 S.W.3d 42 (Tex. 2008) .............................................................................. 9',

        '*Fortune Prod. Co. v. Conoco, Inc.*, 52 S.W.3d 671 (Tex. 2000) ...................... 9',

    for t in st_cases:
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE,

def motion_body(doc):
    # ---- I. Introduction ----
    heading(doc, 'I.  INTRODUCTION')
    body(doc, "This is a breach-of-contract dispute that Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC (\u201cArcadia\u201d) has repackaged as a multi-count tort and statutory action to escape the bargain it struck. Arcadia\u2014a healthcare IT company with approximately $62 million in annual revenue, represented by experienced outside counsel\u2014negotiated and executed a comprehensive $14.7 million Master Software License and Services Agreement (the \u201cMSLSA\u201d) with Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (\u201cMeridian\u201d). The MSLSA is a fully integrated, counseled agreement that allocates risk through an integration clause, a limited warranty with an exclusive remedy, a consequential-damages waiver, and an aggregate-liability cap. When the NexusCore implementation did not meet Arcadia\u2019s expectations, Arcadia filed its First Amended Complaint (Dkt. No. 12) (the \u201cFAC\u201d) demanding $47.3 million\u2014more than three times the contract price\u2014by recasting its contract grievance as fraud, negligent misrepresentation, unjust enrichment, and a violation of the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices\u2013Consumer Protection Act (the \u201cDTPA\u201d).")
    body(doc, "The FAC cannot survive scrutiny. Count V (DTPA) is barred by the DTPA\u2019s own transaction exemption, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code \u00a7 17.49(f), because Arcadia is not an individual and the total consideration ($17.05 million) exceeds the $500,000 threshold by a factor of thirty-four. Count IV (unjust enrichment) is unavailable because an express, valid contract\u2014the MSLSA\u2014governs the same subject matter. Counts II and III (fraud and negligent misrepresentation) fail the particularity requirements of Rule 9(b), run afoul of the economic loss doctrine, rest on non-actionable puffery, and are precluded by the MSLSA\u2019s integration clause and Arcadia\u2019s own due diligence. Count I (breach of contract) is governed by the MSLSA\u2019s deemed-acceptance provision, expired limited warranty, and exclusive-remedy and damages-limitation provisions, which confine any recovery to a fraction of Arcadia\u2019s demand.")
    body(doc, "Meridian therefore moves, pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6), to dismiss all five counts of the FAC with prejudice. Meridian preserved this defense in its Answer (First Affirmative Defense; Part III (Reservation of Rights Regarding Rule 12(b)(6) Motion)), and both the Court\u2019s Standing Order on Motion Practice (\u00a7 3.4) and the Scheduling Order permit this post-answer motion, which is evaluated under the same standard as a pre-answer Rule 12(b)(6) motion.")

    # ---- II. Statement of Facts ----
    heading(doc, 'II.  STATEMENT OF FACTS')
    body(doc, "The facts are drawn from the FAC and from the MSLSA, Statement of Work #1 (\u201cSOW-1\u201d), and the four Change Orders, all of which are referenced throughout the FAC and central to every count. The Court may consider these documents on a Rule 12(b)(6) motion. See \u00a7 III, infra.")
    heading(doc, 'A.  The Parties and the MSLSA.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company with approximately $62 million in annual revenue that operates healthcare analytics platforms for 23 hospitals and more than 140 clinics. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 2, 6. Meridian is a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Austin, Texas, and approximately $380 million in annual revenue. FAC \u00b6 9. Both are sophisticated commercial entities.")
    body(doc, "On March 15, 2022, the parties executed the MSLSA, under which Meridian licensed its NexusCore healthcare analytics module and provided implementation, training, maintenance, support, and data-migration advisory services for a total contract value of $14.7 million. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 33\u201335. The parties concurrently executed SOW-1. FAC \u00b6 35. Arcadia was represented by outside counsel throughout the negotiation, which commenced February 22, 2022. FAC \u00b6 32. The MSLSA designates Delaware law as governing (\u00a7 12.7), contains a comprehensive integration clause (\u00a7 12.1), prohibits oral modifications (\u00a7 12.3), and includes a detailed risk-allocation framework.")
    heading(doc, 'B.  The Contractual Risk-Allocation and Warranty Regime.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "The MSLSA\u2019s Article VIII limits liability. Section 8.1 caps each party\u2019s aggregate liability at \u201cthe total fees paid or payable by Licensee during the twelve (12) month period immediately preceding the event giving rise to such liability,\u201d and provides that the cap \u201cshall apply regardless of the form of action, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise.\u201d Section 8.2 excludes \u201cany indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including but not limited to damages for loss of profits, goodwill, use, data, or other intangible losses.\u201d Section 8.3 designates the Agreement\u2019s remedies as the licensee\u2019s \u201csole and exclusive remedies.\u201d Section 8.5 confirms that these limitations \u201cshall apply even if any limited or exclusive remedy set forth in this Agreement is found to have failed of its essential purpose.\u201d")
    body(doc, "The MSLSA\u2019s acceptance and warranty provisions channel disputes over software performance into defined remedies. Section 5.3 gives the licensee 30 calendar days following deployment to conduct acceptance testing and deliver a written \u201cNonconformity Notice\u201d describing any material nonconformity; if no notice is delivered, the software \u201cshall be deemed accepted.\u201d \u00a7 5.3(a), (d). Production use during the testing period likewise constitutes acceptance. \u00a7 5.3(d). Section 9.1 provides a limited warranty that the software will \u201cperform substantially in accordance with the Documentation\u201d for 90 days following Go-Live, with the \u201csole and exclusive remedy\u201d of repair or replacement\u2014or, if Meridian cannot cure within 60 days, a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees. \u00a7 9.1(a)\u2013(b). Section 9.4 disclaims all other warranties, states that the software is provided \u201cAS IS\u201d and \u201cAS AVAILABLE,\u201d and confirms that the licensee \u201chas relied on its own due diligence and evaluation in selecting the Licensed Software.\u201d")
    body(doc, "SOW-1 allocates data migration to Arcadia. Section 3.2 of SOW-1 provides that Arcadia \u201cshall be solely responsible for all data migration activities,\u201d and that Meridian \u201cshall have no responsibility for the accuracy, completeness, or integrity of migrated data.\u201d Exhibit C, \u00a7 C.2 confirms that Meridian\u2019s data-migration role is advisory only.")
    heading(doc, 'C.  The Implementation and Arcadia\u2019s Acceptance.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "The FAC alleges that Go-Live occurred on January 15, 2023. FAC \u00b6 45. Under \u00a7 5.3, the 30-day acceptance testing period therefore closed on February 14, 2023. The FAC does not allege that Arcadia delivered any written Nonconformity Notice during that period. To the contrary, the FAC\u2019s first reference to any written complaint is Dr. Okonkwo\u2019s March 8, 2023 email\u201422 days after the acceptance window closed\u2014reporting \u201cintermittent latency issues\u201d and \u201coccasional report generation errors.\u201d FAC \u00b6 50. On the FAC\u2019s own allegations, then, acceptance was deemed granted no later than February 14, 2023. \u00a7 5.3(d).")
    heading(doc, 'D.  The Pre-Sale Communications and Arcadia\u2019s Due Diligence.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "The FAC\u2019s fraud and misrepresentation claims rest on pre-sale statements by Meridian\u2019s sales team, principally Account Executive James Poletti, during an October 12, 2021 presentation, a November 18, 2021 demonstration, and a December 9, 2021 written proposal. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 22\u201328. The statements include that NexusCore \u201cdelivers industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics,\u201d that clients \u201ctypically see 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency,\u201d that NexusCore \u201cwill integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform,\u201d and that the platform was \u201cproven,\u201d \u201cpurpose-built,\u201d and could be implemented within approximately six months. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 23, 25, 28, 79.")
    body(doc, "The FAC itself acknowledges facts that defeat any claim of justifiable reliance. The FAC admits that Meridian\u2019s December 9, 2021 written proposal \u201ccontained certain general disclaimers.\u201d FAC \u00b6 27. The FAC admits that Arcadia\u2019s CTO, Martin Schreiber, \u201cconducted certain independent due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients.\u201d FAC \u00b6 31. And the FAC concedes that Arcadia \u201cengaged outside counsel to assist in the negotiation process.\u201d FAC \u00b6 32. These admissions\u2014coupled with the MSLSA\u2019s integration clause\u2014are fatal to Arcadia\u2019s reliance allegations.")
    body(doc, "Finally, the FAC\u2019s allegations of Meridian\u2019s knowledge and concealment are pleaded \u201con information and belief\u201d and are unaccompanied by any stated factual basis. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 56\u201360. As explained below, such conclusory averments cannot satisfy Rule 9(b) or the *Iqbal* plausibility standard.")

    # ---- III. Legal Standard ----
    heading(doc, 'III.  LEGAL STANDARD')
    body(doc, "A Rule 12(b)(6) motion tests the sufficiency of the complaint. To survive, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to \u201cstate a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.\u201d *Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly*, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007); *Ashcroft v. Iqbal*, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). \u201cThreadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.\u201d *Iqbal*, 556 U.S. at 678. The Court \u201caccept[s] all well-pleaded facts as true, viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff,\u201d but is \u201cnot bound to accept as true a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.\u201d *Lormand v. US Unwired, Inc.*, 565 F.3d 228, 255\u201356 (5th Cir. 2009).")
    body(doc, "On a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, the Court may consider documents \u201creferred to in the plaintiff\u2019s complaint and central to the plaintiff\u2019s claim\u201d without converting the motion into one for summary judgment. *Lormand*, 565 F.3d at 255\u201357. The MSLSA, SOW-1, and the four Change Orders are referenced throughout the FAC and are central to every count; the December 9, 2021 proposal is referenced in FAC \u00b6 27 and is central to the fraud and reliance allegations. The Court may therefore consider these documents in ruling on this motion.")
    body(doc, "Claims sounding in fraud must also satisfy the heightened pleading requirements of Rule 9(b), which requires a plaintiff to \u201cstate with particularity the circumstances constituting fraud.\u201d Fed. R. Civ. P. 9(b). The Fifth Circuit requires a plaintiff to \u201cspecify the statements contended to be fraudulent, identify the speaker, state when and where the statements were made, and explain why the statements were fraudulent.\u201d *Benchmark Elecs., Inc. v. J.M. Huber Corp.*, 343 F.3d 719, 724 (5th Cir. 2003). This Court rigorously enforces Rule 9(b). Standing Order \u00a7 4.2.")
    print("motion_body part A loaded OK")
PYEOF

The statements include that NexusCore \u201cdelivers industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics,\u201d that clients \u201ctypically see 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency,\u201d that NexusCore \u201cwill integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform,\u201d and that the platform was \u201cproven,\u201d \u201cpurpose-built,\u201d and could be implemented within approximately six months. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 23, 25, 28, 79.")
    body(doc, "The FAC itself acknowledges facts that defeat any claim of justifiable reliance. The FAC admits that Meridian\u2019s December 9, 2021 written proposal \u201ccontained certain general disclaimers.\u201d FAC \u00b6 27. The FAC admits that Arcadia\u2019s CTO, Martin Schreiber, \u201cconducted certain independent due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients.\u201d FAC \u00b6 31. And the FAC concedes that Arcadia \u201cengaged outside counsel to assist in the negotiation process.\u201d FAC \u00b6 32. These admissions\u2014coupled with the MSLSA\u2019s integration clause\u2014are fatal to Arcadia\u2019s reliance allegations.")
    body(doc, "Finally, the FAC\u2019s allegations of Meridian\u2019s knowledge and concealment are pleaded \u201con information and belief\u201d and are unaccompanied by any stated factual basis. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 56\u201360. As explained below, such conclusory averments cannot satisfy Rule 9(b) or the *Iqbal* plausibility standard.")

    # ---- III. Legal Standard ----
    heading(doc, 'III.  LEGAL STANDARD')
    body(doc, "A Rule 12(b)(6) motion tests the sufficiency of the complaint. To survive, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to \u201cstate a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.\u201d *Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly*, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007); *Ashcroft v. Iqbal*, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). \u201cThreadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.\u201d *Iqbal*, 556 U.S. at 678. The Court \u201caccept[s] all well-pleaded facts as true, viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff,\u201d but is \u201cnot bound to accept as true a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.\u201d *Lormand v. US Unwired, Inc.*, 565 F.3d 228, 255\u201356 (5th Cir. 2009).")
    body(doc, "On a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, the Court may consider documents \u201creferred to in the plaintiff\u2019s complaint and central to the plaintiff\u2019s claim\u201d without converting the motion into one for summary judgment. *Lormand*, 565 F.3d at 255\u201357. The MSLSA, SOW-1, and the four Change Orders are referenced throughout the FAC and are central to every count; the December 9, 2021 proposal is referenced in FAC \u00b6 27 and is central to the fraud and reliance allegations. The Court may therefore consider these documents in ruling on this motion.")
    body(doc, "Claims sounding in fraud must also satisfy the heightened pleading requirements of Rule 9(b), which requires a plaintiff to \u201cstate with particularity the circumstances constituting fraud.\u201d Fed. R. Civ. P. 9(b). The Fifth Circuit requires a plaintiff to \u201cspecify the statements contended to be fraudulent, identify the speaker, state when and where the statements were made, and explain why the statements were fraudulent.\u201d *Benchmark Elecs., Inc. v. J.M. Huber Corp.*, 343 F.3d 719, 724 (5th Cir. 2003). This Court rigorously enforces Rule 9(b). Standing Order \u00a7 4.2.")
    print("motion_body part A loaded OK")
PYEOF

tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cat >> build_docs.py << 'PYEOF'

    # ---- IV. Argument ----
    heading(doc, 'IV.  ARGUMENT')

    # A. Count V (DTPA)
    heading(doc, 'A.  Count V (DTPA) Is Barred by the Section 17.49(f) Transaction Exemption.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Count V should be dismissed first because it is barred by statute. Section 17.49(f) of the DTPA exempts from the Act \u201ca cause of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than $500,000,\u201d unless the consumer is an individual. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code \u00a7 17.49(f). Both conditions for the exemption are satisfied here.")
    body(doc, "First, Arcadia is not an individual. Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company. FAC \u00b6 6. The DTPA\u2019s $500,000 transaction exemption applies to non-individual consumers. See *PPG Indus., Inc. v. JMB/Houston Ctrs. Partners Ltd.*, 146 S.W.3d 79, 89 (Tex. App.\u2014Houston [1st Dist.] 2004, no pet.) (the exemption \u201cwas enacted to prevent sophisticated commercial parties engaged in high-value transactions from using the DTPA\u2014with its treble damages provision\u2014as a weapon in ordinary commercial disputes\u201d).")
    body(doc, "Second, the total consideration vastly exceeds $500,000. The MSLSA\u2019s total contract value is $14.7 million. FAC \u00b6 34. The four Change Orders added $2.35 million, for total consideration of $17.05 million. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 46\u201347. That figure exceeds the statutory threshold by a factor of more than thirty-four. The exemption is determined by the total consideration involved in the transaction, not by the amount of damages claimed. *PPG Indus.*, 146 S.W.3d at 89.")
    body(doc, "Arcadia therefore cannot be a \u201cconsumer\u201d capable of invoking the DTPA for this transaction. The FAC\u2019s DTPA claim\u2014including its demand for treble damages and attorneys\u2019 fees under \u00a7\u00a7 17.50(b)(1), (d)\u2014fails as a matter of law. Count V should be dismissed with prejudice. In any event, the alleged statements on which Count V rests are non-actionable puffery (see \u00a7 IV.C.2), and Arcadia cannot establish that any such statement was a producing cause of its damages where, on the FAC\u2019s own allegations, Arcadia accepted the software and continued to use it. See *Melody Home Mfg. Co. v. Barnes*, 741 S.W.2d 349, 351 (Tex. 1987).")

    # B. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment)
    heading(doc, 'B.  Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) Is Barred by the Express Contract.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Count IV fails because unjust enrichment is unavailable where an express contract governs the subject matter of the dispute. \u201cRecovery under quantum meruit [or unjust enrichment] is not available when there is a valid, express contract covering the subject matter of the dispute.\u201d *Fortune Prod. Co. v. Conoco, Inc.*, 52 S.W.3d 671, 684 (Tex. 2000). The Texas Supreme Court has reiterated that \u201can action for unjust enrichment is \u2018not proper\u2019 where \u2018the same subject\u2019 is covered by an express agreement between the parties.\u201d *Excess Underwriters at Lloyd\u2019s v. Frank\u2019s Casing Crew & Rental Tools, Inc.*, 246 S.W.3d 42, 59 (Tex. 2008) (quoting *Fortune Prod.*).")
    body(doc, "The FAC pleads itself out of court on this claim. In Count I, Arcadia alleges that \u201ca valid and enforceable written contract\u2014the MSLSA and accompanying SOW-1\u201d exists and was breached by Meridian. FAC \u00b6 69. Arcadia does not contend that the MSLSA is void or unenforceable; to the contrary, Arcadia\u2019s entire breach-of-contract claim depends on the MSLSA\u2019s validity. The MSLSA indisputably covers the \u201csame subject matter\u201d\u2014the licensing, implementation, training, support, and data-migration advisory services at issue. *Excess Underwriters*, 246 S.W.3d at 59\u201360 (the \u201csame subject matter\u201d test is applied broadly).")
    body(doc, "Arcadia\u2019s only basis for pleading unjust enrichment \u201cin the alternative\u201d is its assertion that the claim applies \u201cto the extent the MSLSA or any provision thereof is found to be unenforceable.\u201d FAC \u00b6 99. But Arcadia does not actually allege that the MSLSA is unenforceable; it sues on it. And the Texas Supreme Court has squarely rejected the argument that contractual limitations on recovery create a \u201cgap\u201d that unjust enrichment can fill. *Excess Underwriters*, 246 S.W.3d at 59 (\u201cequity follows the law\u201d\u2014where parties have allocated risk through contract, equity will not rewrite the bargain). The fact that the MSLSA\u2019s limitation of liability (\u00a7 8.1) and consequential-damages waiver (\u00a7 8.2) may limit Arcadia\u2019s contract recovery does not entitle Arcadia to circumvent those provisions through an equitable claim. Count IV should be dismissed.")

    # C. Count II (Fraud)
    heading(doc, 'C.  Count II (Fraud) Fails Under Rule 9(b), the Economic Loss Doctrine, the Puffery Doctrine, and the Integration Clause.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Count II fails on at least four independent grounds.")
    heading(doc, '1.  The FAC does not satisfy Rule 9(b).', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Rule 9(b) requires Arcadia to plead the \u201cwho, what, when, where, and why\u201d of the alleged fraud. *Benchmark Elecs.*, 343 F.3d at 724. The FAC identifies the speaker (Poletti), the approximate dates (October\u2013November 2021), and the content of the statements. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 22\u201325, 79. But it fails the \u201cwhy\u201d prong: it does not allege facts showing that the statements were false when made. Instead, the FAC reasons backwards\u2014because NexusCore allegedly performed poorly after Go-Live in January 2023, the pre-sale statements must have been false in October\u2013November 2021. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 79\u201380. That is not enough. \u201cAllegations of fraud based on post-hoc product or service failures do not, without more, support an inference that the defendant knew its pre-sale representations were false at the time they were made.\u201d *Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Fund, Inc. v. TXU Corp.*, 565 F.3d 200, 207 (5th Cir. 2009).")
    body(doc, "The FAC\u2019s scienter allegations are equally deficient. The FAC alleges that Meridian\u2019s sales team \u201cknew\u201d the representations were false or made them \u201crecklessly.\u201d FAC \u00b6 81. But Rule 9(b), although permitting scienter to be alleged generally, still requires factual allegations giving rise to a \u201cstrong inference\u201d of fraudulent intent. *Flaherty*, 565 F.3d at 207. The FAC alleges no internal Meridian documents, no contemporaneous admissions, and no specific facts from which one could infer that Poletti knew, in October 2021, that NexusCore could not perform substantially as described. The FAC\u2019s \u201cknowledge\u201d and \u201cconcealment\u201d allegations are pleaded \u201con information and belief\u201d (FAC \u00b6\u00b6 56\u201360) and are unaccompanied by any stated factual basis\u2014precisely the type of pleading the Fifth Circuit and this Court\u2019s Standing Order (\u00a7 4.2) hold insufficient. See *Benchmark*, 343 F.3d at 724.")
    heading(doc, '2.  The alleged statements are non-actionable puffery.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Even accepting the FAC\u2019s characterization of the statements, they are non-actionable puffery. \u201cA general claim of superiority that is so vague that it can be understood only as the seller\u2019s opinion of the product rather than a specific, factual representation\u201d constitutes puffery. *Pizza Hut, Inc. v. Papa John\u2019s Int\u2019l, Inc.*, 227 F.3d 489, 497 (5th Cir. 2000). Statements that a product delivers \u201cindustry-leading performance,\u201d that clients \u201ctypically see 30-40% improvement,\u201d that integration will be \u201cseamless,\u201d and that the product is \u201cproven\u201d and \u201cpurpose-built\u201d are classic general claims of product superiority and projected benefits. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 23, 25, 28, 79. They are \u201cexaggerated advertising, blustering, and boasting upon which no reasonable buyer would rely.\u201d *Castrol Inc. v. Pennzoil Co.*, 987 F.2d 939, 945 (3d Cir. 1993). Puffery remains non-actionable even when embedded in a sales presentation that includes other content. *Pizza Hut*, 227 F.3d at 498\u201399.")
    body(doc, "This is especially true where, as here, the buyer is a sophisticated commercial entity. A sophisticated buyer \u201cis in at least as good a position\u201d as the seller to evaluate general promotional statements and cannot reasonably rely on them. *Presidio Enters., Inc. v. Warner Bros. Distrib. Corp.*, 784 F.2d 674, 680 (5th Cir. 1986). Arcadia\u2014a $62-million company advised by counsel\u2014cannot transform sales puffery into fraud by repeating it in a complaint.")
    heading(doc, '3.  The economic loss doctrine bars the fraud claim.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "The economic loss doctrine bars tort claims that seek only economic losses arising from a contractual relationship. Under Delaware law (which governs the MSLSA, \u00a7 12.7), \u201cwhere a plaintiff\u2019s claims arise solely from the contractual relationship between the parties, and the damages sought are exclusively economic losses, the economic loss doctrine bars recovery in tort.\u201d *Brasby v. Morris Dynamics, Inc.*, 947 A.2d 1042, 1049 (Del. 2008). The doctrine applies with particular force where sophisticated parties have negotiated a comprehensive written agreement with an integration clause and warranty disclaimers. *Kuhn Constr., Inc. v. Diamond State Port Corp.*, 990 A.2d 393, 401\u201302 (Del. 2010). Even under Texas\u2019s more limited economic loss rule, tort claims are barred where \u201c(1) the plaintiff\u2019s losses are purely economic, (2) the losses relate to the subject matter of a contract between the parties, and (3) the defendant owes no duty independent of the contract.\u201d *Chapman Custom Homes, Inc. v. Dallas Plumbing Co.*, 445 S.W.3d 716, 718\u201319 (Tex. 2014); see *Sharyland Water Supply Corp. v. City of Alton*, 354 S.W.3d 407, 415\u201316 (Tex. 2011).")
    body(doc, "All three conditions are met. Arcadia\u2019s damages\u2014$14.7 million in contract value, $18.4 million in lost profits, $6.7 million in operating costs, and $5 million in reputational harm\u2014are purely economic. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 61\u201366. They relate entirely to NexusCore, the subject of the MSLSA. And the alleged misrepresentations concern the quality and performance of NexusCore\u2014the very subject matter of the contract. Arcadia\u2019s fraud claim is, at bottom, a claim that Meridian did not deliver software that performed as promised\u2014\u201cthe very essence of a breach of contract claim.\u201d *Sharyland*, 354 S.W.3d at 415. Meridian owes Arcadia no duty independent of the MSLSA. The fraud claim is barred under the law of both jurisdictions.")
    heading(doc, '4.  The integration clause and Arcadia\u2019s own conduct defeat justifiable reliance.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Even if the economic loss doctrine did not apply, Arcadia cannot establish justifiable reliance. The MSLSA\u2019s integration clause provides that the Agreement \u201cconstitutes the entire agreement between the Parties\u201d and \u201csupersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written,\u201d and that each party \u201cacknowledges that, in entering into this Agreement, it has not relied upon any statement, representation, warranty, or agreement of the other Party except as expressly set forth in this Agreement.\u201d \u00a7 12.1. Under Delaware law, \u201ca party to a contract cannot promise, in a clear integration clause of a negotiated agreement, that it is not relying on promises or representations made by the other party outside of the agreement, and then assert a claim for fraud based on those very representations.\u201d *Eagle Indus., Inc. v. DeVilbiss Health Care, Inc.*, 702 A.2d 1228, 1232 (Del. 1997); see *SIGA Techs., Inc. v. PharmAthene, Inc.*, 67 A.3d 330, 344 (Del. 2013). Arcadia\u2019s fraud claim rests entirely on extra-contractual pre-sale representations that \u00a7 12.1 was designed to supersede.")
    body(doc, "The FAC\u2019s own allegations confirm the absence of justifiable reliance. Arcadia admits that Meridian\u2019s December 9, 2021 proposal \u201ccontained certain general disclaimers.\u201d FAC \u00b6 27. Arcadia admits that its CTO \u201cconducted certain independent due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients.\u201d FAC \u00b6 31. And Arcadia admits that it \u201cengaged outside counsel to assist in the negotiation process.\u201d FAC \u00b6 32. Under Texas law, a plaintiff cannot establish justifiable reliance where it conducted an independent investigation and possessed information bearing on the alleged misrepresentation. *Hollcroft & Sedgewick, L.L.P. v. Pac. Mut. Life Ins. Co.*, 51 S.W.3d 573, 577 (Tex. 2001). Arcadia\u2014counseled, sophisticated, and having conducted its own due diligence\u2014cannot claim it was defrauded by general sales statements that the MSLSA\u2019s integration clause superseded. Count II should be dismissed.")
    print("motion_body part B (Counts V, IV, II) loaded OK")
PYEOF

Arcadia\u2019s damages\u2014$14.7 million in contract value, $18.4 million in lost profits, $6.7 million in operating costs, and $5 million in reputational harm\u2014are purely economic. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 61\u201366. They relate entirely to NexusCore, the subject of the MSLSA. And the alleged misrepresentations concern the quality and performance of NexusCore\u2014the very subject matter of the contract. Arcadia\u2019s fraud claim is, at bottom, a claim that Meridian did not deliver software that performed as promised\u2014\u201cthe very essence of a breach of contract claim.\u201d *Sharyland*, 354 S.W.3d at 415. Meridian owes Arcadia no duty independent of the MSLSA. The fraud claim is barred under the law of both jurisdictions.")
    heading(doc, '4.  The integration clause and Arcadia\u2019s own conduct defeat justifiable reliance.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Even if the economic loss doctrine did not apply, Arcadia cannot establish justifiable reliance. The MSLSA\u2019s integration clause provides that the Agreement \u201cconstitutes the entire agreement between the Parties\u201d and \u201csupersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written,\u201d and that each party \u201cacknowledges that, in entering into this Agreement, it has not relied upon any statement, representation, warranty, or agreement of the other Party except as expressly set forth in this Agreement.\u201d \u00a7 12.1. Under Delaware law, \u201ca party to a contract cannot promise, in a clear integration clause of a negotiated agreement, that it is not relying on promises or representations made by the other party outside of the agreement, and then assert a claim for fraud based on those very representations.\u201d *Eagle Indus., Inc. v. DeVilbiss Health Care, Inc.*, 702 A.2d 1228, 1232 (Del. 1997); see *SIGA Techs., Inc. v. PharmAthene, Inc.*, 67 A.3d 330, 344 (Del. 2013). Arcadia\u2019s fraud claim rests entirely on extra-contractual pre-sale representations that \u00a7 12.1 was designed to supersede.")
    body(doc, "The FAC\u2019s own allegations confirm the absence of justifiable reliance. Arcadia admits that Meridian\u2019s December 9, 2021 proposal \u201ccontained certain general disclaimers.\u201d FAC \u00b6 27. Arcadia admits that its CTO \u201cconducted certain independent due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients.\u201d FAC \u00b6 31. And Arcadia admits that it \u201cengaged outside counsel to assist in the negotiation process.\u201d FAC \u00b6 32. Under Texas law, a plaintiff cannot establish justifiable reliance where it conducted an independent investigation and possessed information bearing on the alleged misrepresentation. *Hollcroft & Sedgewick, L.L.P. v. Pac. Mut. Life Ins. Co.*, 51 S.W.3d 573, 577 (Tex. 2001). Arcadia\u2014counseled, sophisticated, and having conducted its own due diligence\u2014cannot claim it was defrauded by general sales statements that the MSLSA\u2019s integration clause superseded. Count II should be dismissed.")
    print("motion_body part B (Counts V, IV, II) loaded OK")
