{"command": "cat >> build_docs.py << 'PYEOF'

    # D. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation)
    heading(doc, 'D.  Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) Fails for Lack of an Independent Duty and Under the Economic Loss Doctrine.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Count III fails for three reasons.")
    body(doc, "First, Arcadia cannot identify a duty independent of the MSLSA. Texas recognizes negligent misrepresentation under Restatement (Second) of Torts \u00a7 552, but liability is \u201cconstrained by the independent duty requirement.\u201d *McCamish, Martin, Brown & Loeffler v. F.E. Appling Interests*, 991 S.W.2d 787, 791\u201392 (Tex. 1999). Meridian\u2019s relationship with Arcadia is purely contractual; the MSLSA defines Meridian\u2019s obligations regarding software performance through its limited warranty (\u00a7 9.1) and warranty disclaimer (\u00a7 9.4). There is no fiduciary relationship, no professional duty, and no statutory duty independent of the contract. Arcadia\u2019s negligent misrepresentation claim is simply a repackaged contract claim.")
    body(doc, "Second, the economic loss doctrine bars the claim for the same reasons discussed in Section IV.C.3. Arcadia\u2019s losses are purely economic, relate to the subject of the MSLSA, and arise from the contractual relationship. *Chapman Custom Homes*, 445 S.W.3d at 718\u201319; *Brasby*, 947 A.2d at 1049.")
    body(doc, "Third, Arcadia\u2019s damages are benefit-of-the-bargain losses recoverable only in contract. The Texas Supreme Court has held that negligent misrepresentation is available only for \u201cout-of-pocket\u201d losses, and that where the plaintiff\u2019s true complaint is that the other party \u201cdid not deliver what was contractually promised, the claim sounds in contract, not tort.\u201d *Fed. Land Bank Ass\u2019n v. Sloane*, 825 S.W.2d 439, 442\u201343 (Tex. 1992). Arcadia seeks the $14.7 million contract value, $18.4 million in lost profits, and $6.7 million in operating costs\u2014all contract damages. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 62\u201364. Under *Sloane*, the claim fails.")
    body(doc, "Finally, to the extent Count III sounds in fraud, it must satisfy Rule 9(b). *Benchmark Elecs.*, 343 F.3d at 724 (Rule 9(b) applies to \u201call cases where the gravamen of the claim is fraud\u201d). For the reasons stated in Section IV.C.1, it does not. Count III should be dismissed.")

    # E. Count I (Breach of Contract)
    heading(doc, 'E.  Count I (Breach of Contract) Is Barred by Deemed Acceptance, the Expired Limited Warranty, and the Contractual Damages Limitations.', space_before=6, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Count I should be dismissed, or in the alternative, Arcadia\u2019s damages should be limited to the contractual remedies.")
    heading(doc, '1.  Deemed acceptance bars rejection-based theories.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Section 5.3 of the MSLSA gave Arcadia 30 calendar days following Go-Live to conduct acceptance testing and deliver a written Nonconformity Notice. \u00a7 5.3(a), (c). The FAC alleges that Go-Live occurred on January 15, 2023. FAC \u00b6 45. The acceptance window therefore closed on February 14, 2023. The FAC does not allege that Arcadia delivered any Nonconformity Notice during that period; the FAC\u2019s first reference to any written complaint is Dr. Okonkwo\u2019s March 8, 2023 email\u201422 days late. FAC \u00b6 50. Under \u00a7 5.3(d), the software \u201cshall be deemed accepted\u201d when the licensee fails to deliver timely notice. Section 5.3(d) further provides that production use during the testing period constitutes acceptance\u2014and the FAC alleges that Arcadia used NexusCore in production. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 49, 55.")
    body(doc, "Deemed acceptance is enforceable and provides a \u201cbright-line rule.\u201d *Simulados, Inc. v. Canton Health Mgmt. Co.*, 2019 WL 4573218, at *5 (W.D. Tex. Sept. 20, 2019) (enforcing a 30-day deemed-acceptance provision where the licensee failed to deliver written notice); *Precision Healthcare Solutions v. Nextera Data Sys.*, 458 F. Supp. 3d 544, 551 (N.D. Tex. 2020) (deemed acceptance applies to both patent and latent defects). On the FAC\u2019s own allegations, Arcadia accepted the software no later than February 14, 2023. Arcadia\u2019s post-acceptance complaints are governed by the warranty provisions, not by any theory of rejection or fundamental breach.")
    heading(doc, '2.  The limited warranty expired, and its remedy is exclusive.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Section 9.1\u2019s limited warranty ran for 90 days following Go-Live\u2014i.e., through April 15, 2023. \u00a7 9.1(a). The \u201csole and exclusive remedy\u201d for breach of that warranty is repair or replacement, or, if Meridian cannot cure within 60 days, a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees. \u00a7 9.1(b). Section 8.3 confirms that the Agreement\u2019s remedies are the licensee\u2019s \u201csole and exclusive remedies.\u201d Section 9.4 disclaims all other warranties and confirms the software is provided \u201cAS IS.\u201d Arcadia\u2019s demand for the full $14.7 million contract value as \u201crestitution\u201d (FAC \u00b6 62) is unavailable where Arcadia accepted the software and where the contract provides an exclusive remedy. See *Kana Software, Inc. v. Sealand Tech., Inc.*, 178 A.3d 1045, 1058\u201360 (Del. Ch. 2017) (where the contract provides a specific remedy for nonconformance, that remedy is exclusive and precludes other damage theories).")
    heading(doc, '3.  In the alternative, the contractual damages limitations bar the bulk of Arcadia\u2019s demand.', space_before=4, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Even if some breach-of-contract theory survived, Arcadia\u2019s $47.3 million demand is barred in large part by the MSLSA\u2019s damages limitations. Section 8.2 waives \u201cany indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including but not limited to damages for loss of profits, goodwill, use, data, or other intangible losses.\u201d Arcadia\u2019s claims for $18.4 million in lost profits, $6.7 million in increased operating costs, $5 million in reputational harm, and $2.5 million in punitive/DTPA damages are consequential or intangible losses expressly waived. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 63\u201366. Section 8.1 caps aggregate liability at the fees paid or payable in the 12 months preceding the event giving rise to liability\u2014a fraction of the $47.3 million demand. And \u00a7 8.5 confirms that these limitations apply \u201ceven if any limited or exclusive remedy ... is found to have failed of its essential purpose.\u201d")
    body(doc, "Limitation-of-liability clauses and consequential-damages waivers in negotiated commercial software agreements between sophisticated parties are enforceable. *Dresser-Rand Co. v. Virtual Automation, Inc.*, 361 F.3d 831, 838\u201340 (5th Cir. 2004). Arcadia was represented by counsel during negotiations and attempted\u2014unsuccessfully\u2014to modify the limitation-of-liability provisions, FAC \u00b6 32, demonstrating that the risk allocation was deliberate and bargained-for. Under *Dresser-Rand*, the Court should enforce \u00a7\u00a7 8.1 and 8.2 and confine any recovery to the contractual limits.")
    body(doc, "For these reasons, Count I should be dismissed. In the alternative, all damages theories exceeding the contractual remedies\u2014including all consequential damages and any amount above the \u00a7 8.1 aggregate-liability cap\u2014should be dismissed.")

    # ---- V. Conclusion ----
    heading(doc, 'V.  CONCLUSION')
    body(doc, "For the foregoing reasons, Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. respectfully requests that the Court grant this motion and dismiss all five counts of Plaintiff\u2019s First Amended Complaint with prejudice. In the alternative, as to Count I, Meridian requests that the Court dismiss all damages theories that exceed the MSLSA\u2019s exclusive remedies and damages limitations, including all claims for consequential, punitive, and exemplary damages and any amount above the \u00a7 8.1 aggregate-liability cap.")

    # ---- Signature ----
    blank(doc)
    signers = [
        {'name': 'Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway', 'bar': 'Texas State Bar No. 24037891', 'email': 'mcalloway@stonebridgecalloway.com'},
        {'name': 'David Arsenault', 'bar': 'Texas State Bar No. 24098254', 'email': 'darsenault@stonebridgecalloway.com'},
    ]
    sig_block(doc, 'STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP', signers, 'January 12, 2024',
              'Attorneys for Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.')

    # ---- Certificate of Service ----
    blank(doc)
    heading(doc, 'CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE', space_before=12, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "I hereby certify that on January 12, 2024, I electronically filed the foregoing Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\u2019s Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff\u2019s First Amended Complaint Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) with the Clerk of Court using the CM/ECF system, which will send notification of such filing to all counsel of record, including:")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "Jonathan Breckenridge")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "Wexford Hale LLP")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "300 West 6th Street, Suite 1500")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "Austin, TX 78701")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "Email: jbreckenridge@wexfordhale.com")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "Counsel for Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC", italic_default=True)
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "/s/ Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway")

    # ---- Certificate of Conference ----
    blank(doc)
    heading(doc, 'CERTIFICATE OF CONFERENCE', space_before=12, space_after=4)
    body(doc, "Pursuant to the Court\u2019s Standing Order on Motion Practice (\u00a7 3.1), I certify that on January 9, 2024, I conferred in good faith by telephone with Jonathan Breckenridge, counsel for Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC, regarding Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\u2019s intent to file the foregoing Motion to Dismiss. During that conference, I advised Mr. Breckenridge of the grounds for the motion and inquired whether Plaintiff would voluntarily dismiss or narrow any of the claims asserted in the First Amended Complaint. Mr. Breckenridge declined to dismiss or narrow any claim. The parties were unable to reach agreement on the relief sought, and the nature of the motion\u2014which presents legal questions concerning the sufficiency of the First Amended Complaint under Rules 8, 9(b), and 12(b)(6) and the application of the MSLSA\u2019s contractual provisions\u2014is such that no compromise was practicable.")
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "/s/ Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway")
    print("motion_body complete loaded OK")
PYEOF

text: Now the proposed order and the internal cover memo, plus the main routine to save and validate everything.

{"command": "cat >> build_docs.py << 'PYEOF'

# ============================ PROPOSED ORDER ============================
def build_order():
    doc = Document()
    base_style(doc); set_margins(doc); add_page_numbers(doc)
    center(doc, 'UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT', bold=True)
    center(doc, 'WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS', bold=True)
    center(doc, 'AUSTIN DIVISION', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'ARCADIA HEALTH SYSTEMS, LLC,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tPlaintiff,')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'v.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.,')
    center(doc, '\t\t\tDefendant.')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'Case No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA')
    blank(doc)
    center(doc, 'ORDER', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    body(doc, "On this day came on to be considered Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.\u2019s Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff\u2019s First Amended Complaint Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) [Dkt. No. __], and the Court, having reviewed the motion, the First Amended Complaint [Dkt. No. 12], and the applicable law, is of the opinion that the motion should be granted.")
    body(doc, "It is therefore ORDERED, ADJUDGED, and DECREED that:")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "1.  Defendant\u2019s Motion to Dismiss [Dkt. No. __] is GRANTED.")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "2.  All claims and causes of action asserted by Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC against Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. in the First Amended Complaint [Dkt. No. 12]\u2014including Count I (Breach of Contract), Count II (Fraud), Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation), Count IV (Unjust Enrichment), and Count V (Violation of the Texas Deceptive Trade Practices\u2013Consumer Protection Act)\u2014are DISMISSED WITH PREJUDICE.")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "3.  All other relief requested by Plaintiff and not expressly granted herein is DENIED.")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE, left_indent=0.5)
    rich(p, "4.  This Order is a final judgment that disposes of all claims and all parties and is appealable. The Clerk of Court is directed to close this case.")
    blank(doc)
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.DOUBLE)
    rich(p, "SIGNED this _____ day of ________________, 2024.")
    blank(doc)
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "__________________________________________")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "THE HONORABLE CATHERINE M. ALVAREZ")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "UNITED STATES DISTRICT JUDGE")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS")
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, "AUSTIN DIVISION")
    print("build_order loaded OK")
    return doc

# ============================ COVER MEMO ============================
def build_memo():
    doc = Document()
    base_style(doc); set_margins(doc); add_page_numbers(doc)
    # Letterhead
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, 'STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP', bold=True)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, '600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800  \u2022  Austin, TX 78701  \u2022  (512) 555-4200')
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, 'PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  \u2014  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, 'MEMORANDUM', bold=True)
    blank(doc)
    # header fields
    def field(label, val):
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
        r = p.add_run(label); r.bold = True; _set_run_font(r)
        rich(p, '  ' + val)
    field('TO:', 'Margaret \u201cMeg\u201d Calloway, Lead Partner')
    field('FROM:', 'David Arsenault, Senior Associate')
    field('DATE:', 'January 11, 2024')
    field('RE:', 'Threshold Issues and Filing Checklist \u2014 Defendant\u2019s Rule 12(b)(6) Motion to Dismiss, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Division)')
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.CENTER, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    pPr = p._p.get_or_add_pPr(); pbdr = OxmlElement('w:pBdr'); bottom = OxmlElement('w:bottom')
    bottom.set(qn('w:val'),'single'); bottom.set(qn('w:sz'),'6'); bottom.set(qn('w:space'),'1'); bottom.set(qn('w:color'),'auto')
    pbdr.append(bottom); pPr.append(pbdr)
    blank(doc)

    def mh(t):  # memo heading
        heading(doc, t, space_before=8, space_after=4)
    def mb(t):  # memo body (single-spaced for internal memo readability)
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, space_after=6)
        rich(p, t)
        return p
    def mb_indent(t):
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.3, space_after=6)
        rich(p, t)
        return p

    mh('I.  Purpose')
    mb("This memorandum flags threshold issues that must be resolved before we file Meridian\u2019s Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss (the \u201cMotion\u201d), currently scheduled for filing on January 12, 2024, ahead of the January 15, 2024 dispositive-motion deadline set by the Scheduling Order. The most pressing issue\u2014a likely defect in the Court\u2019s subject-matter jurisdiction\u2014could affect whether we file the Motion at all. The remaining items are practical/filing matters that need your sign-off before we docket.")

    mh('II.  CRITICAL \u2014 Likely Defect in Diversity Jurisdiction')
    mb("Bottom line: there is a substantial likelihood that complete diversity of citizenship does not exist and that the Court lacks subject-matter jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. \u00a7 1332(a). This issue was flagged in the December 18, 2023 research memo (\u00a7 XV) and is confirmed by Arcadia\u2019s operating agreement produced in discovery. It must be resolved before we file.")
    mb("The analysis. Meridian is a citizen of Delaware (state of incorporation) and Texas (principal place of business, Austin). Notice of Removal \u00b6\u00b6 9\u201311; FAC \u00b6 9. Arcadia is a limited liability company, and an LLC\u2019s citizenship is determined by the citizenship of all of its members, traced through all tiers of ownership. *Harvey v. Grey Wolf Drilling Co.*, 542 F.3d 1077, 1080 (5th Cir. 2008); *Carden v. Arkoma Assocs.*, 494 U.S. 185, 195\u201396 (1990). The Notice of Removal asserted that Arcadia is a citizen of Texas only, based solely on managing member Dr. Okonkwo. Notice of Removal \u00b6\u00b6 12\u201314. That is legally incorrect: it failed to trace citizenship through all members.")
    mb("Per the operating agreement (Exhibit A), Arcadia\u2019s three members are: (1) Dr. Rachel Okonkwo (55%) \u2014 a Texas citizen; (2) Martin Schreiber (15%) \u2014 a Texas citizen; and (3) Apex Medical Ventures, LP (30%) \u2014 a Delaware limited partnership. Apex\u2019s partners are: (a) Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc. \u2014 a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Wilmington, Delaware \u2014 a citizen of Delaware; and (b) the Okonkwo Family Trust \u2014 a Texas trust whose sole trustee and primary beneficiary (Dr. Okonkwo) is domiciled in Texas \u2014 a citizen of Texas. Arcadia\u2019s citizenship is therefore Texas and Delaware. Meridian\u2019s citizenship is Delaware and Texas. Both parties share citizenship in Delaware and Texas. Complete diversity is destroyed. *Strawbridge v. Curtiss*, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806).")
    mb("Note on the trust. The research memo described the Okonkwo Family Trust as \u201cirrevocable,\u201d but the operating agreement (Exhibit A, Note 3) describes it as a \u201crevocable inter vivos trust\u201d with Dr. Okonkwo as sole trustee and primary beneficiary. This discrepancy does not change the outcome: under either characterization the trust is a Texas citizen (settlor, trustee, and primary beneficiary are all domiciled in Texas). Either way, Arcadia is a Delaware citizen through Apex\u2019s general partner, and that alone destroys diversity with Meridian. We should still obtain the trust instrument and the Apex partnership agreement to confirm, but the defect appears irreparable.")
    mb("Consequences. Subject-matter jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be raised by the Court sua sponte at any time. *Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp.*, 546 U.S. 500, 514 (2006). If the defect is as described, the case must be remanded to the 73rd District Court, Bexar County, Texas. We removed this case on the (defective) diversity theory, so proceeding with a merits motion while aware of a likely jurisdictional defect raises candor-to-the-tribunal concerns.")
    mb("Options. (1) File the Motion and do not raise jurisdiction \u2014 not recommended; the Court may notice the defect sua sponte (Judge Alvarez is rigorous), remand, and render the Motion moot, and we have a duty of candor that likely requires disclosure. (2) File the Motion and concurrently disclose the jurisdictional issue (a short notice or jurisdictional memorandum) \u2014 most candid, but may prompt remand and loss of the federal forum. (3) Move to remand before filing the Motion \u2014 candid and may be strategically sound if state court is acceptable, but we lose the federal-forum advantages. (4) Investigate further before deciding \u2014 confirm the Apex partnership agreement and trust instrument; assess whether any basis exists to establish complete diversity (unlikely, given the Delaware general partner).")
    mb("Forum trade-offs. Federal court (Judge Alvarez) offers rigorous pleading standards (favoring our 12(b)(6)), a bench trial (the Scheduling Order already enforced the \u00a7 12.9 jury waiver), and a sophisticated commercial context. State court (Bexar County) may treat DTPA and fraud claims more sympathetically and may contest the \u00a7 12.9 jury waiver, though Texas courts also enforce integration clauses, liability caps, and the economic loss doctrine. The jury waiver is a meaningful advantage we currently enjoy; remand risks losing it.")
    mb("Recommendation. Resolve the citizenship analysis definitively before filing. If the defect is confirmed (as appears likely), we should make a deliberate, documented decision about remand versus proceeding, and at minimum be prepared to address jurisdiction. My preliminary view: the defect is serious enough that we should not file a merits motion that ignores it. This needs your decision before January 12.")
    print("build_memo part 1 loaded OK")
    return doc
PYEOF

{"command": "cat >> build_docs.py << 'PYEOF'

def memo_body(doc):
    def mh(t): heading(doc, t, space_before=8, space_after=4)
    def mb(t):
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, space_after=6)
        rich(p, t); return p
    def mbi(t):
        p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.JUSTIFY, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE, left_indent=0.3, space_after=6)
        rich(p, t); return p

    mh('III.  Other Threshold and Practical Issues')
    mb("1.  Post-answer 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c). The Standing Order (\u00a7 3.4) says the Court will treat a preserved post-answer 12(b)(6) as a 12(b)(6) motion; the Scheduling Order says the Court \u201cwill treat any such motion as a Rule 12(c) motion ... evaluated under the same standard as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion.\u201d The standard is identical; the only practical difference is that 12(c) permits consideration of the Answer. We preserved the defense in the Answer (First Affirmative Defense; Part III). The Motion is titled 12(b)(6) per the Standing Order\u2019s caption example. No action needed, but be aware the Court may label it 12(c).")
    mb("2.  Count I \u2014 dismissal vs. damages limitation. Full dismissal of a breach-of-contract claim at the pleading stage is aggressive. The deemed-acceptance and warranty-expiration arguments are strong but may be viewed as turning on the application of contract terms to facts. The Motion presents full dismissal as the primary request and damages limitation (\u00a7\u00a7 8.1, 8.2) as the alternative. The alternative is the safer landing spot if the Court is reluctant to dismiss Count I entirely. Confirm we are comfortable with this posture.")
    mb("3.  Matters outside the pleadings \u2014 conversion risk. Several of our strongest facts are NOT in the FAC and appear only in our Answer and discovery: Poletti\u2019s contemporaneous notes (showing his actual statement was qualified, not \u201cseamless\u201d); the 45-day project-manager gap; the 75-day delay in API specifications; and Linden Park Consulting\u2019s data-migration errors. Relying on these at 12(b)(6) would risk conversion to summary judgment under Rule 12(d). The Motion is deliberately limited to (a) the FAC\u2019s own allegations, (b) the MSLSA, SOW-1, and Change Orders (referenced in and central to the FAC \u2014 consider-able under *Lormand*), and (c) the December 9, 2021 proposal (referenced in FAC \u00b6 27). The Poletti notes, PM gap, API delay, and Linden Park errors are preserved for summary judgment. Please confirm the brief adheres to this limit.")
    mb("4.  Choice of law. \u00a7 12.7 selects Delaware law for the contract. The Motion applies Delaware law to Count I and argues the tort claims fail under BOTH Delaware and Texas law (economic loss doctrine and integration clause under Delaware; puffery, reliance, and independent-duty under Texas). Confirm we are comfortable arguing in the alternative under both bodies of law.")
    mb("5.  \u00a7 8.1 cap \u2014 \u201cevent giving rise to liability\u201d ambiguity. The cap is \u201cfees paid or payable during the 12 months preceding the event giving rise to liability.\u201d The \u201cevent\u201d is ambiguous (pre-sale misrepresentations? Go-Live? first complaint?). We need Meridian\u2019s billing records to quantify the cap. The Motion argues the cap applies without specifying a dollar figure. Flag: obtain billing records so we can quantify the cap if the Court reaches the damages-limitation alternative.")
    mb("6.  \u00a7 8.4(e) willful-misconduct exception. The \u00a7\u00a7 8.1/8.2 limitations do not apply to \u201cwillful misconduct or gross negligence\u201d (\u00a7 8.4(e)). Arcadia alleges \u201cknowing and intentional\u201d conduct (FAC \u00b6\u00b6 88, 111) \u2014 directed at the fraud/DTPA claims. Our tort-dismissal arguments (economic loss, puffery, Rule 9(b)) dispose of the torts before the cap is reached. For Count I (pure contract breach), the cap and waiver apply; \u00a7 8.4(e) is not implicated. The Motion handles this correctly \u2014 the damages-limitation argument is framed for Count I. Confirm.")
    mb("7.  Certificate of conference \u2014 separate docketing and actual conferral. The Standing Order (\u00a7 3.1) requires the certificate of conference to be filed as a SEPARATE document concurrently with the Motion. The draft includes it as a section at the end of the Motion for convenience; we should docket it as a separate entry. Critically, we must actually confer with Mr. Breckenridge by telephone (not email alone) before filing and document the date and substance. The draft certificate reflects a January 9, 2024 telephone conference \u2014 confirm this occurred, or schedule it before filing.")
    mb("8.  Page limits. The Motion is limited to 25 pages (Standing Order \u00a7 3.2; Scheduling Order). Reply briefs are limited to 10 pages per the Scheduling Order, which controls over the Standing Order\u2019s 15-page reply limit for this case. The draft should be reviewed for length; trim if it exceeds 25 pages of substantive content.")
    mb("9.  Proposed order \u2014 docket number. Per Standing Order \u00a7 3.5, the proposed order must identify the Motion by docket entry number. We will not know the number until filing. The draft uses \u201c[Dkt. No. __]\u201d; we must update it within one business day of filing.")
    mb("10. Bar numbers. The Notice of Removal and the Answer list different Texas Bar numbers for you and me. The Answer (most recent) lists Calloway 24037891 and Arsenault 24098254; the Notice of Removal lists 24078153 and 24112490. The Motion uses the Answer\u2019s numbers. Please confirm the correct bar numbers so we do not file with erroneous credentials.")
    mb("11. Jury-waiver status. The Scheduling Order already provides that the parties waived jury trial under \u00a7 12.9 and the case will be tried to the bench. This favors us (no jury on the DTPA/punitive claims). If the case is remanded (see \u00a7 II), this benefit may be lost or contested in state court \u2014 another factor in the remand analysis.")

    mh('IV.  Recommendation and Next Steps')
    mb("1.  Decide the jurisdictional question (\u00a7 II) before filing. This is the gating issue.")
    mb("2.  Confirm the actual conferral with opposing counsel and docket the certificate of conference separately.")
    mb("3.  Confirm bar numbers.")
    mb("4.  Final length check on the Motion (\u2264 25 pages substantive).")
    mb("5.  File on or before January 15, 2024; update the proposed order\u2019s docket number within one business day.")
    blank(doc)
    mb("Please advise on the jurisdictional issue at your earliest convenience. I am happy to circulate a short jurisdictional memorandum or a draft motion to remand if you would like to pursue that path.")
    blank(doc)
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, '/s/ David Arsenault')
    p = para(doc, align=WD_ALIGN_PARAGRAPH.LEFT, line=WD_LINE_SPACING.SINGLE)
    rich(p, 'David Arsenault')
    print("memo_body complete loaded OK")

# ============================ MAIN ============================
def main():
    import os
    out = 'output'
    os.makedirs(out, exist_ok=True)
    # Motion
    d = build_motion()
    motion_body(d)
    d.save(os.path.join(out, 'motion-to-dismiss.docx'))

    # Order

    # Memo
    d3 = build_memo()
    memo_body(d3)
    d3.save(os.path.join(out, 'cover-memo.docx'))
    print('saved cover-memo.docx')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

        r'a plaintiff cannot aggregate generalized assertions of fraud. *Dorsey v. Portfolio '
        r'Equities, Inc.*, 540 F.3d 333, 340 (5th Cir. 2008). The FAC does precisely that, '

        r'\u201cfraudulent inducements.\u201d FAC \u00b6\u00b6 22\u201328, 78.")')

325:    body(doc, "A Rule 12(b)(6) motion tests the sufficiency of the complaint. To survive, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to \u201cstate a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.\u201d *Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly*, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007); *Ashcroft v. Iqbal*, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). \u201cThreadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.\u201d *Iqbal*, 556 U.S. at 678. The Court \u201caccept[s] all well-pleaded facts as true, viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff,\u201d but is \u201cnot bound to accept as true a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.\u201d *Lormand v. 325:    body(doc, "A Rule 12(b)(6) motion tests the sufficiency of the complaint. To survive, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to \u201cstate a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.\u201d *Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly*, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007); *Ashcroft v. Iqbal*, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). \u201cThreadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.\u201d *Iqbal*, 556 U.S. at 678. The Court \u201caccept[s] all well-pleaded facts as true, viewing them in the light most favorable to the plaintiff,\u201d but is \u201cnot bound to accept as true a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation.\u201d *Lormand v. US Unwired, Inc.*, 565 F.3d 228, 255\u201356 (5th Cir. 2009).")
350:    body(doc, "Rule 9(b) requires Arcadia to plead the \u201cwho, what, when, where, and why\u201d of the alleged fraud. *Benchmark Elecs.*, 343 F.3d at 724. The FAC identifies the speaker (Poletti), the approximate dates (October\u2013November 2021), and the content of the statements. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 22\u201325, 79. But it fails the \u201cwhy\u201d prong: it does not allege facts showing that the statements were false when made. Instead, the FAC reasons backwards\u2014because NexusCore allegedly performed poorly after Go-Live in January 2023, the pre-sale statements must have been false in October\u2013November 2021. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 79\u201380. That is not enough. \u201cAllegations of fraud based on post-hoc product or service failures do not, without more, support an inference that the defendant knew its pre-sale representations were false at the time they were made.\u201d *Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Fund, Inc. v. TXU Corp.*, 565 F.3d 200, 207 (5th Cir. 2009).")
--- context around each ---

350:    body(doc, "Rule 9(b) requires Arcadia to plead the \u201cwho, what, when, where, and why\u201d of the alleged fraud. *Benchmark Elecs.*, 343 F.3d at 724. The FAC identifies the speaker (Poletti), the approximate dates (October\u2013November 2021), and the content of the statements. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 22\u201325, 79. But it fails the \u201cwhy\u201d prong: it does not allege facts showing that the statements were false when made. Instead, the FAC reasons backwards\u2014because NexusCore allegedly performed poorly after Go-Live in January 2023, the pre-sale statements must have been false in October\u2013November 2021. FAC \u00b6\u00b6 79\u201380. That is not enough. \u201cAllegations of fraud based on post-hoc product or service failures do not, without more, support an inference that the defendant knew its pre-sale representations were false at the time they were made.\u201d *Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Fund, Inc. v. TXU Corp.*, 565 F.3d 200, 207 (5th Cir. 2009).")
351:    body(doc, "The FAC\u2019s scienter allegations are equally deficient. The FAC alleges that Meridian\u2019s sales team \u201cknew\u201d the representations were false or made them \u201crecklessly.\u201d FAC \u00b6 81. But Rule 9(b), although permitting scienter to be alleged generally, still requires factual allegations giving rise to a \u201cstrong inference\u201d of fraudulent intent. *Flaherty*, 565 F.3d at 207. The FAC alleges no internal Meridian documents, no contemporaneous admissions, and no specific facts from which one could infer that Poletti knew, in October 2021, that NexusCore could not perform substantially as described. The FAC\u2019s \u201cknowledge\u201d and \u201cconcealment\u201d allegations are pleaded \u201con information and belief\u201d (FAC \u00b6\u00b6 56\u201360) and are unaccompanied by any stated factual basis\u2014precisely the type of pleading the Fifth Circuit and this Court\u2019s Standing Order (\u00a7 4.2) hold insufficient. See *Benchmark*, 343 F.3d at 724.")

335:    body(doc, "Count V should be dismissed first because it is barred by statute. 366:    body(doc, "First, Arcadia cannot identify a duty independent of the MSLSA. Texas recognizes negligent misrepresentation under Restatement (Second) of Torts \u00a7 552, but liability is \u201cconstrained by the independent duty requirement.\u201d *McCamish, Martin, Brown & Loeffler v. F.E. Appling Interests*, 991 S.W.2d 787, 791\u201392 (Tex. 1999). Meridian\u2019s relationship with Arcadia is purely contractual; the MSLSA defines Meridian\u2019s obligations regarding software performance through its limited warranty (\u00a7 9.1) and warranty disclaimer (\u00a7 9.4).      On this day came on to be considered Defendant Meridian Cloud

Solutions, Inc.’s Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff’s First Amended Complaint

Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) [Dkt. No. __], and the

Court, having reviewed the motion, the First Amended Complaint [Dkt. No.

12], and the applicable law, is of the opinion that the motion should be

granted.

     It is therefore ORDERED, ADJUDGED, and DECREED that:

     1. Defendant’s Motion to Dismiss [Dkt. No. __] is GRANTED.

     2. All claims and causes of action asserted by Plaintiff Arcadia Health

     Systems, LLC against Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. in the



                                     1

----PAGE----
     First Amended Complaint [Dkt. No. 12]—including Count I (Breach of

     Contract), Count II (Fraud), Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation),

     Count IV (Unjust Enrichment), and Count V (Violation of the Texas

     Deceptive Trade Practices–Consumer Protection Act)—are

     DISMISSED WITH PREJUDICE.

     3. All other relief requested by Plaintiff and not expressly granted

     herein is DENIED.

     4. This Order is a final judgment that disposes of all claims and all

     parties and is appealable. The Clerk of Court is directed to close this

     case.



SIGNED this _____ day of ________________, 2024.



__________________________________________
THE HONORABLE CATHERINE M. ALVAREZ
UNITED STATES DISTRICT JUDGE
WESTERN DISTRICT OF TEXAS
AUSTIN DIVISION




                                     2

----PAGE----

############ COVER MEMO (first 2 pages) ############
                 STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP
 600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800 • Austin, TX 78701 • (512) 555-4200

  PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT

                             MEMORANDUM

TO: Margaret “Meg” Calloway, Lead Partner
FROM: David Arsenault, Senior Associate
DATE: January 11, 2024
RE: Threshold Issues and Filing Checklist — Defendant’s Rule 12(b)(6)
Motion to Dismiss, Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud
Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Division)




I. Purpose

This memorandum flags threshold issues that must be resolved before we
file Meridian’s Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss (the “Motion”), currently
scheduled for filing on January 12, 2024, ahead of the January 15, 2024
dispositive-motion deadline set by the Scheduling Order. The most pressing
issue—a likely defect in the Court’s subject-matter jurisdiction—could affect
whether we file the Motion at all. The remaining items are practical/filing
matters that need your sign-off before we docket.
II. CRITICAL — Likely Defect in Diversity Jurisdiction

Bottom line: there is a substantial likelihood that complete diversity of
citizenship does not exist and that the Court lacks subject-matter
jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a). This issue was flagged in the
December 18, 2023 research memo (§ XV) and is confirmed by Arcadia’s
operating agreement produced in discovery. It must be resolved before we
file.
The analysis. Meridian is a citizen of Delaware (state of incorporation) and
Texas (principal place of business, Austin). Notice of Removal ¶¶ 9–11; FAC
¶ 9. Arcadia is a limited liability company, and an LLC’s citizenship is
determined by the citizenship of all of its members, traced through all tiers
of ownership. Harvey v. Grey Wolf Drilling Co. , 542 F.3d 1077, 1080 (5th
Cir. 2008); Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185, 195–96 (1990). The
Notice of Removal asserted that Arcadia is a citizen of Texas only, based
solely on managing member Dr. Okonkwo. Notice of Removal ¶¶ 12–14.
That is legally incorrect: it failed to trace citizenship through all members.




                                      1

----PAGE----
Per the operating agreement (Exhibit A), Arcadia’s three members are: (1)
Dr. Rachel Okonkwo (55%) — a Texas citizen; (2) Martin Schreiber (15%) —
a Texas citizen; and (3) Apex Medical Ventures, LP (30%) — a Delaware
limited partnership. Apex’s partners are: (a) Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc.
— a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in
Wilmington, Delaware — a citizen of Delaware; and (b) the Okonkwo Family
Trust — a Texas trust whose sole trustee and primary beneficiary (Dr.
Okonkwo) is domiciled in Texas — a citizen of Texas. Arcadia’s citizenship is
therefore Texas and Delaware. Meridian’s citizenship is Delaware and
Texas. Both parties share citizenship in Delaware and Texas. Complete
diversity is destroyed. Strawbridge v. Curtiss, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806).
Note on the trust. The research memo described the Okonkwo Family Trust
as “irrevocable,” but the operating agreement (Exhibit A, Note 3) describes
it as a “revocable inter vivos trust” with Dr. Okonkwo as sole trustee and
primary beneficiary. This discrepancy does not change the outcome: under
either characterization the trust is a Texas citizen (settlor, trustee, and
primary beneficiary are all domiciled in Texas). Either way, Arcadia is a
Delaware citizen through Apex’s general partner, and that alone destroys
diversity with Meridian. We should still obtain the trust instrument and the
Apex partnership agreement to confirm, but the defect appears irreparable.
Consequences. Subject-matter jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be
raised by the Court sua sponte at any time. Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp., 546
U.S. 500, 514 (2006). If the defect is as described, the case must be
remanded to the 73rd District Court, Bexar County, Texas. We removed this
case on the (defective) diversity theory, so proceeding with a merits motion

federal-forum advantages. (4) Investigate further before deciding — confirm
the Apex partnership agreement and trust instrument; assess whether any
basis exists to establish complete diversity (unlikely, given the Delaware
general partner).
Forum trade-offs. Federal court (Judge Alvarez) offers rigorous pleading
standards (favoring our 12(b)(6)), a bench trial (the Scheduling Order
already enforced the § 12.9 jury waiver), and a sophisticated commercial
context. State court (Bexar County) may treat DTPA and fraud claims more
sympathetically and may contest the § 12.9 jury waiver, though Texas courts


                                     2

----PAGE----
also enforce integration clauses, liability caps, and the economic loss
doctrine. The jury waiver is a meaningful advantage we currently enjoy;
remand risks losing it.
Recommendation. Resolve the citizenship analysis definitively before filing.
If the defect is confirmed (as appears likely), we should make a deliberate,
documented decision about remand versus proceeding, and at minimum be
prepared to address jurisdiction. My preliminary view: the defect is serious
enough that we should not file a merits motion that ignores it. This needs
your decision before January 12.
III. Other Threshold and Practical Issues

1. Post-answer 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c). The Standing Order (§ 3.4) says the Court
will treat a preserved post-answer 12(b)(6) as a 12(b)(6) motion; the
Scheduling Order says the Court “will treat any such motion as a Rule 12(c)
motion ... evaluated under the same standard as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion.”
The standard is identical; the only practical difference is that 12(c) permits
consideration of the Answer. We preserved the defense in the Answer (First
Affirmative Defense; Part III). The Motion is titled 12(b)(6) per the Standing
Order’s caption example. No action needed, but be aware the Court may
label it 12(c).
2. Count I — dismissal vs. damages limitation. Full dismissal of a breach-of-
contract claim at the pleading stage is aggressive. The deemed-acceptance
and warranty-expiration arguments are strong but may be viewed as
turning on the application of contract terms to facts. The Motion presents
full dismissal as the primary request and damages limitation (§§ 8.1, 8.2) as
the alternative. The alternative is the safer landing spot if the Court is
reluctant to dismiss Count I entirely. Confirm we are comfortable with this
posture.
3.   Matters outside the pleadings — conversion risk. Several of our
strongest facts are NOT in the FAC and appear only in our Answer and
discovery: Poletti’s contemporaneous notes (showing his actual statement
was qualified, not “seamless”); the 45-day project-manager gap; the 75-day
delay in API specifications; and Linden Park Consulting’s data-migration
errors. Relying on these at 12(b)(6) would risk conversion to summary
judgment under Rule 12(d). The Motion is deliberately limited to (a) the
FAC’s own allegations, (b) the MSLSA, SOW-1, and Change Orders
(referenced in and central to the FAC — consider-able under Lormand), and
(c) the December 9, 2021 proposal (referenced in FAC ¶ 27). The Poletti
notes, PM gap, API delay, and Linden Park errors are preserved for
summary judgment. Please confirm the brief adheres to this limit.
4. Choice of law. § 12.7 selects Delaware law for the contract. The Motion
applies Delaware law to Count I and argues the tort claims fail under BOTH
Delaware and Texas law (economic loss doctrine and integration clause


                                      3

----PAGE----
under Delaware; puffery, reliance, and independent-duty under Texas).
Confirm we are comfortable arguing in the alternative under both bodies of
law.
5. § 8.1 cap — “event giving rise to liability” ambiguity. The cap is “fees
paid or payable during the 12 months preceding the event giving rise to
liability.” The “event” is ambiguous (pre-sale misrepresentations? Go-Live?
first complaint?). We need Meridian’s billing records to quantify the cap.
The Motion argues the cap applies without specifying a dollar figure. Flag:
obtain billing records so we can quantify the cap if the Court reaches the
damages-limitation alternative.
6. § 8.4(e) willful-misconduct exception. The §§ 8.1/8.2 limitations do not
apply to “willful misconduct or gross negligence” (§ 8.4(e)). Arcadia alleges
“knowing and intentional” conduct (FAC ¶¶ 88, 111) — directed at the
fraud/DTPA claims. Our tort-dismissal arguments (economic loss, puffery,
Rule 9(b)) dispose of the torts before the cap is reached. For Count I (pure
contract breach), the cap and waiver apply; § 8.4(e) is not implicated. The
Motion handles this correctly — the damages-limitation argument is framed
for Count I. Confirm.
7. Certificate of conference — separate docketing and actual conferral. The
Standing Order (§ 3.1) requires the certificate of conference to be filed as a
SEPARATE document concurrently with the Motion. The draft includes it as
a section at the end of the Motion for convenience; we should docket it as a
separate entry. Critically, we must actually confer with Mr. Breckenridge by
telephone (not email alone) before filing and document the date and
substance. The draft certificate reflects a January 9, 2024 telephone
conference — confirm this occurred, or schedule it before filing.
8. Page limits. The Motion is limited to 25 pages (Standing Order § 3.2;
Scheduling Order). Reply briefs are limited to 10 pages per the Scheduling
Order, which controls over the Standing Order’s 15-page reply limit for this
case. The draft should be reviewed for length; trim if it exceeds 25 pages of
substantive content.
9. Proposed order — docket number. Per Standing Order § 3.5, the
proposed order must identify the Motion by docket entry number. We will
not know the number until filing. The draft uses “[Dkt. No. __]”; we must
update it within one business day of filing.
10. Bar numbers. The Notice of Removal and the Answer list different Texas
Bar numbers for you and me. The Answer (most recent) lists Calloway
24037891 and Arsenault 24098254; the Notice of Removal lists 24078153
and 24112490. The Motion uses the Answer’s numbers. Please confirm the
correct bar numbers so we do not file with erroneous credentials.
11. Jury-waiver status. The Scheduling Order already provides that the
parties waived jury trial under § 12.9 and the case will be tried to the
bench. This favors us (no jury on the DTPA/punitive claims). If the case is


                                      4

----PAGE----
remanded (see § II), this benefit may be lost or contested in state court —
another factor in the remand analysis.
IV. Recommendation and Next Steps

1. Decide the jurisdictional question (§ II) before filing. This is the gating
issue.
2. Confirm the actual conferral with opposing counsel and docket the
certificate of conference separately.
3. Confirm bar numbers.
4. Final length check on the Motion (≤ 25 pages substantive).
5. File on or before January 15, 2024; update the proposed order’s docket
number within one business day.

Please advise on the jurisdictional issue at your earliest convenience. I am
happy to circulate a short jurisdictional memorandum or a draft motion to
remand if you would like to pursue that path.

/s/ David Arsenault
David Arsenault




                                      5

----PAGE----

############ MOTION: signature + certificates (last 2 pages) ############

----PAGE----
      The MSLSA’s acceptance and warranty provisions channel disputes

over software performance into defined remedies. Section 5.3 gives the

licensee 30 calendar days following deployment to conduct acceptance

testing and deliver a written “Nonconformity Notice” describing any

material nonconformity; if no notice is delivered, the software “shall be

deemed accepted.” § 5.3(a), (d). Production use during the testing period

likewise constitutes acceptance. § 5.3(d). Section 9.1 provides a limited

warranty that the software will “perform substantially in accordance with

the Documentation” for 90 days following Go-Live, with the “sole and

exclusive remedy” of repair or replacement—or, if Meridian cannot cure

within 60 days, a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees. § 9.1(a)–(b).

Section 9.4 disclaims all other warranties, states that the software is

provided “AS IS” and “AS AVAILABLE,” and confirms that the licensee “has

relied on its own due diligence and evaluation in selecting the Licensed

Software.”

      SOW-1 allocates data migration to Arcadia. Section 3.2 of SOW-1

provides that Arcadia “shall be solely responsible for all data migration

activities,” and that Meridian “shall have no responsibility for the accuracy,

completeness, or integrity of migrated data.” Exhibit C, § C.2 confirms that

Meridian’s data-migration role is advisory only.

C. The Implementation and Arcadia’s Acceptance.

      The FAC alleges that Go-Live occurred on January 15, 2023. FAC ¶ 45.

Under § 5.3, the 30-day acceptance testing period therefore closed on


                                      7

----PAGE----
February 14, 2023. The FAC does not allege that Arcadia delivered any

written Nonconformity Notice during that period. To the contrary, the

FAC’s first reference to any written complaint is Dr. Okonkwo’s March 8,

2023 email—22 days after the acceptance window closed—reporting

“intermittent latency issues” and “occasional report generation errors.”

FAC ¶ 50. On the FAC’s own allegations, then, acceptance was deemed

granted no later than February 14, 2023. § 5.3(d).

D. The Pre-Sale Communications and Arcadia’s Due Diligence.

      The FAC’s fraud and misrepresentation claims rest on pre-sale

statements by Meridian’s sales team, principally Account Executive James

Poletti, during an October 12, 2021 presentation, a November 18, 2021

demonstration, and a December 9, 2021 written proposal. FAC ¶¶ 22–28.

The   statements    include    that    NexusCore    “delivers   industry-leading

performance for healthcare analytics,” that clients “typically see 30-40%

improvement in reporting efficiency,” that NexusCore “will integrate

seamlessly with any EHR platform,” and that the platform was “proven,”

“purpose-built,” and could be implemented within approximately six

months. FAC ¶¶ 23, 25, 28, 79.

      The FAC itself acknowledges facts that defeat any claim of justifiable

src = open('build_docs.py', encoding='utf-8').read()

from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Pt, Inches
d = Document('output/motion-to-dismiss.docx')
s = d.sections[0]
print("Margins (in): top=%.2f bottom=%.2f left=%.2f right=%.2f" % (
    s.top_margin.inches, s.bottom_margin.inches, s.left_margin.inches, s.right_margin.inches))
n = d.styles['Normal']
print("Normal font:", n.font.name, "size:", n.font.size.pt if n.font.size else None)
print("Normal line spacing rule:", n.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule)
# footer check
f = s.footer.paragraphs[0]
print("Footer align:", f.alignment, "| has PAGE field:", 'PAGE' in f.paragraphs[0]._p.xml if False else 'instrText' in s.footer.paragraphs[0]._p.xml)
# count paragraphs / sample a body paragraph spacing
import collections
ls = collections.Counter()
for p in d.paragraphs:
    ls[str(p.paragraph_format.line_spacing_rule)] += 1
print("Line-spacing distribution:", dict(ls))
print("Total paragraphs:", len(d.paragraphs))
PYEOF
echo "=== Final file listing ==="
ls -la output/"}

{"file_path": "response.md", "content": "# Rule 12(b)(6) Motion Package — Work Summary

**Matter:** *Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.*, No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Division; Hon. Catherine M. Alvarez)

**Deliverables (all in `output/`, validated against ECMA-376 / OOXML):**

| File | Pages | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `motion-to-dismiss.docx` | 25 | Defendant's Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss all five counts of the First Amended Complaint |
| `proposed-order.docx` | 2 | Self-executing proposed order granting the motion (separate filing per Standing Order § 3.5) |
| `cover-memo.docx` | 5 | Privileged internal memo flagging threshold issues for the lead partner before filing |

## How the documents were built

I read the full case file — the First Amended Complaint, the MSLSA and SOW-1, Meridian's Answer, the defense research/case-law memo, the Notice of Removal, the Scheduling Order, Judge Alvarez's Standing Order, and Arcadia's operating agreement — then generated the three `.docx` files with `python-docx` for precise control over Judge Alvarez's formatting requirements (12-pt Times New Roman, double-spaced, 1-inch margins, page numbers centered in the footer, compliant caption/signature/certificates). Each file was run through `skills/docx/scripts/validate.py` (all pass) and rendered to PDF to confirm page counts and that the Table of Authorities page numbers match the actual citation pages.

## Motion to Dismiss — argument structure

The motion is a post-answer Rule 12(b)(6) motion (defense preserved in Meridian's Answer, First Affirmative Defense and Part III; permitted by the Standing Order § 3.4 and the Scheduling Order). It is limited to the FAC's own allegations plus the MSLSA, SOW-1, Change Orders, and the Dec. 9, 2021 proposal (all referenced in and central to the FAC, so consider-able under *Lormand* without conversion). It deliberately does **not** rely on facts appearing only in the Answer/discovery (Poletti's notes, the PM gap, the API-spec delay, Linden Park's errors) — those are preserved for summary judgment.

- **Count V (DTPA)** — dismissed on the dispositive § 17.49(f) transaction exemption: Arcadia is an LLC (not an individual) and total consideration is $17.05M (> $500K threshold by 34×). *PPG Industries.*
- **Count IV (Unjust Enrichment)** — barred because an express contract (the MSLSA) governs the same subject matter; Arcadia sues *on* the contract and does not allege it is void. *Fortune Production; Excess Underwriters.*
- **Count II (Fraud)** — four independent grounds: (1) Rule 9(b) "why" failure (post-hoc product failure ≠ falsity when made; "on information and belief" knowledge allegations lack a factual basis; *Benchmark; Flaherty; Dorsey*); (2) non-actionable puffery ("industry-leading," "30-40% improvement," "seamless," "proven"; *Pizza Hut; Castrol; Presidio*); (3) economic loss doctrine under both Delaware (*Brasby; Kuhn*) and Texas (*Sharyland; Chapman*) law; (4) integration clause / no justifiable reliance (§ 12.1; Arcadia's own admissions of disclaimers, due diligence, and counsel; *Eagle Industries; SIGA; Hollcroft*).
- **Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation)** — no independent duty (*McCamish*); economic loss doctrine; benefit-of-the-bargain damages not recoverable in tort (*Sloane*); Rule 9(b) applies (*Benchmark*).
- **Count I (Breach of Contract)** — primary: deemed acceptance under § 5.3 (Go-Live 1/15/23 → 30-day window closed 2/14/23; first written complaint 3/8/23, 22 days late; *Simulados; Precision Healthcare*) and the expired, exclusive § 9.1 warranty (*Kana Software*). Alternative: §§ 8.1/8.2/8.5 cap and waive the consequential damages that make up the bulk of the $47.3M demand (*Dresser-Rand*).

Choice of law is handled in the alternative: Delaware governs the contract (§ 12.7); the tort claims are argued to fail under **both** Delaware and Texas law. A note in the Legal Standard satisfies Standing Order § 1.4 (binding vs. persuasive authority). The brief includes a certificate of conference (Jan. 9, 2024 telephone conferral) and certificate of service.

## Cover Memo — threshold issues flagged

The lead issue is a **likely defect in subject-matter jurisdiction**. The Notice of Removal asserted Arcadia is a citizen of Texas only (based on managing member Okonkwo), but an LLC's citizenship runs through *all* members (*Harvey; Carden*). Per the operating agreement, Arcadia's members include Apex Medical Ventures, LP (a Delaware LP) whose general partner is a Delaware corporation — so Arcadia is a citizen of **both Texas and Delaware**, the same two states as Meridian (Delaware incorporation + Texas principal place of business). Complete diversity appears destroyed (*Strawbridge*); jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be raised sua sponte (*Arbaugh*). The memo lays out the analysis, the irrevocable/revocable-trust discrepancy (which does not change the outcome), the candor-to-the-tribunal implications of proceeding, the federal-vs.-state forum trade-offs (including the § 12.9 jury waiver already enforced in federal court), and four options (proceed / disclose concurrently / move to remand / investigate further), with a recommendation to resolve the citizenship question before filing.

The memo also flags: the 12(b)(6)-vs.-12(c) labeling conflict between the Standing and Scheduling Orders; the aggressive posture of fully dismissing Count I (with damages limitation as the safer alternative); the Rule 12(d) conversion risk; choice-of-law strategy; the § 8.1 "event giving rise to liability" ambiguity (needs billing records); the § 8.4(e) willful-misconduct exception; the requirement to docket the certificate of conference separately and to actually confer by phone; the 25-page / 10-page-reply limits; the need to update the proposed order's docket number after filing; a bar-number discrepancy between the Notice of Removal and the Answer; and the jury-waiver status.

## Notes for the filer

- The proposed order uses `[Dkt. No. __]` placeholders that must be updated with the motion's docket number within one business day of filing (Standing Order § 3.5).
- The certificate of conference is included in the motion for convenience but should be docketed as a **separate** document (Standing Order § 3.1); confirm the actual telephone conferral with opposing counsel occurred.
- Bar numbers in the motion follow the Answer (Calloway 24037891; Arsenault 24098254); confirm against firm records given the discrepancy with the Notice of Removal.
- The jurisdictional issue in the cover memo is gating and should be resolved before the January 15, 2024 deadline."}

tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd /workspace
echo "=== Final deliverables ==="
ls -la output/*.docx
echo ""
echo "=== Final validation ==="
for f in motion-to-dismiss proposed-order cover-memo; do
  printf "%-22s " "$f.docx:" && python3 skills/docx/scripts/validate.py output/$f.docx
done
echo ""
echo "=== Page counts (PDF render) ==="
for f in motion-to-dismiss proposed-order cover-memo; do
  soffice --headless --convert-to pdf --outdir /tmp output/$f.docx >/dev/null 2>&1
  printf "%-22s " "$f:" && pdfinfo /tmp/$f.pdf 2>/dev/null | awk '/Pages/{print $2 " pages"}'
done
echo ""
echo "=== Motion substantive-content page check (excludes caption/TOC/TOA p1-3 & sig/certs p24-25) ==="
echo "Substantive argument spans pages 4-23 = 20 pages (limit: 25) -> COMPLIANT""}

text:
All three deliverables are complete, validated, and compliant. Here's a summary of what I produced:

## Deliverables (in `output/`)

**1. `motion-to-dismiss.docx`** (25 pages) — Defendant Meridian's Rule 12(b)(6) motion to dismiss all five counts of the First Amended Complaint, with caption, table of contents, table of authorities (page numbers verified against the actual render), signature block, and certificates of service and conference. It addresses each count:
- **Count V (DTPA)** — dispositive § 17.49(f) transaction exemption ($17.05M consideration; Arcadia is an LLC, not an individual)
- **Count IV (Unjust Enrichment)** — barred by the express MSLSA covering the same subject matter
- **Count II (Fraud)** — Rule 9(b) failure, puffery, economic loss doctrine (Del. & Tex.), and the integration clause/no justifiable reliance
- **Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation)** — no independent duty, economic loss doctrine, benefit-of-the-bargain damages
- **Count I (Breach of Contract)** — deemed acceptance (§ 5.3), expired exclusive warranty (§ 9.1), and (alternative) the § 8.1/8.2 damages limitations

**2. `proposed-order.docx`** (2 pages) — clean, self-executing proposed order dismissing all counts with prejudice, per Standing Order § 3.5 (with `[Dkt. No. __]` placeholders to update after filing).

**3. `cover-memo.docx`** (5 pages) — privileged internal memo flagging the **critical threshold issue**: a likely defect in diversity jurisdiction. The Notice of Removal treated Arcadia as a Texas-only citizen, but tracing through all LLC members (including the Delaware-limited-partnership member whose general partner is a Delaware corporation) makes Arcadia a citizen of *both* Texas and Delaware — the same states as Meridian — destroying complete diversity. The memo also covers 11 other practical/filing issues (post-answer 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c), conversion risk, choice of law, the § 8.1 cap ambiguity, certificate-of-conference docketing, page limits, bar-number discrepancy, etc.).

All files comply with Judge Alvarez's Standing Order (12-pt Times New Roman, double-spaced, 1-inch margins, centered page numbers, compliant caption/signature/certificates; substantive content within the 25-page limit) and pass OOXML validation. A detailed work summary is in `response.md`.
